4. Spread a veil of flour on the work surface, pour the mixture onto it, cover it with more flour and let it take enough flour (something less than 100 grams will do) to form rather soft balls about the size of hazelnuts.
5. Drop them into boiling broth and after 10 minutes of cooking serve them.
6. This dose will serve ten or twelve persons.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestra di Pangrattato (Breadcrumb Soup)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Leftover bread pieces, dried and pounded and sifted into breadcrumbs, in the same proportion as semolina
- Eggs, two or more depending on quantity
- Parmigiano cheese, one heaping spoonful for each egg

## Directions

1. Pour the breadcrumbs into boiling broth in the same proportion as semolina.
2. According to the quantity, beat two or more eggs in the soup tureen, add to it one heaping spoonful of parmigiano for each egg and pour the hot soup into it little by little.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestra di Passatelli (Passatelli Soup)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 100 grams breadcrumbs
- 20 grams beef marrow
- 40 grams grated Parmesan cheese
- 2 eggs
- nutmeg or lemon zest, or both together

## Directions

1. The marrow serves to make them more tender and is not necessary to dissolve it by heat; it suffices to crush it and break it up with the blade of a knife
2. Knead everything together to form a rather firm loaf; but leave aside some breadcrumbs to add later if needed
3. They are called passatelli because they take their special form by being forced through the holes of an iron made expressly for this purpose
4. They can also be passed through a syringe
5. Boil them in broth and serve
6. This amount is sufficient for four people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestra di Passatelli di Carne (Meat Passatelli Soup)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 150 grams beef fillet
- 50 grams breadcrumbs
- 30 grams grated Parmesan cheese
- 15 grams beef marrow
- 15 grams butter
- 2 egg yolks
- salt to taste
- nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Pound the fillet in a mortar and pass it through a sieve
2. Crush the marrow and butter together with the blade of a knife and unite them with the meat
3. Add the rest to make a paste that will be firm enough to press the iron over it like the passatelli from the previous recipe
4. Boil them in broth for ten minutes and serve for six people
5. Also a chicken breast or a piece of turkey breast, boiled or raw, can be used for this purpose instead of the fillet
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestra di Semolino Composta I (Composed Semolina Soup I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fine grain semolina
- Milk, one glass
- Salt, to taste
- Parmigiano cheese, grated
- Butter, a small piece
- Nutmeg, to taste
- Eggs, 2

## Directions

1. Cook fine grain semolina in milk and throw in enough so it turns out quite firm.
2. When you remove it from the fire season it with salt, grated parmigiano, a small piece of butter and a hint of nutmeg and let it cool.
3. Then dissolve the mixture with eggs until you reduce it like a liquid cream.
4. Take a smooth tin mold, grease the bottom well with butter, stick to it an equally greased sheet and pour the said mixture into it to set it in a bain-marie with fire on top.
5. Once cooked and cooled, a knife blade passed all around and the paper at the bottom will help you unmold it.
6. Cut it into small bricks or pieces the thickness of a coin and one or two centimeters wide and throw them into broth making them boil for a few minutes.
7. One glass of milk and two eggs is enough to make soup for four or five people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestra di Semolino Composta II (Composed Semolina Soup II)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- For each egg: Semolina, 30 grams
- Parmigiano cheese, grated, 20 grams
- Butter, 20 grams
- Salt, a pinch
- Nutmeg, to taste

## Directions

1. Dissolve the butter over the fire and, removed from the fire, pour the semolina and parmigiano over it, dissolving the mixture well with the eggs.
2. Then pour it into a saucepan with a buttered sheet underneath to set it between two fires, taking care that it does not brown.
3. Once unmolded and cooled, cut it into small dice or in another way, making it boil in the broth for ten minutes.
4. Three eggs will be enough for five people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Minestrone (Vegetable Soup)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Usual boiled meat
- A handful of fresh shelled beans, or if dried, give them half cooking in water
- Savoy cabbage, spinach and a little beet
- 40 grams fat ham
- One clove garlic
- A pinch of parsley
- Celery
- Carrot
- One potato
- One zucchini
- Salt and pepper
- Broth
- Oil

## Directions

1. Cook first a handful of fresh shelled beans in the broth, or if dried give them half cooking in water.
2. Slice thinly savoy cabbage, spinach and a little beet, keep them soaking in fresh water, then put them in a saucepan dry and when they have water over fire, drain them well by pressing with a spoon.
3. For a soup for four or five people, prepare a battuto with 40 grams of fat ham, one clove of garlic, a pinch of parsley, soften it, then pour it into the said saucepan together with celery, carrot, one potato, one zucchini and very little of [incomplete recipe]
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Mostarda all'Uso Toscano (Tuscan Mustard Preserve)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sweet grapes, 1/3 black and 2/3 white, or all white, 2 kilogramms
- Rose apples or reinette apples, 1 kilogramm
- Two large pears
- White wine, preferably Vin Santo, 240 grams
- Candied citron, 120 grams
- White mustard powder, 40 grams
- Cinnamon powder

## Directions

1. Crush the grapes as you would for wine
2. After one or two days, when it has risen, press out the must
3. Peel the apples and pears and cut them into thin slices
4. Put them on the fire with the said wine and when it has all evaporated, pour in the must
5. Stir frequently and when the mixture is condensed somewhat more than fruit preserve, let it cool
6. Dissolve the mustard powder in a little very hot wine and add it along with the candied citron cut into small pieces
7. Preserve it in small jars with a thin layer of cinnamon powder on top
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Nasello alla Palermitana (Hake Palermo Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Hake (cod), 500 to 600 grams
- Salt
- Pepper
- Oil
- Breadcrumbs
- Rosemary (optional)
- Salted anchovies (3 large or 4 small)
- Lemon

## Directions

1. Take a hake weighing 500 to 600 grams, remove all the fins except the tail fin, leaving the head on.
2. Split it along the belly to remove the innards and the spine, flatten it, and season with a little salt and pepper.
3. Turn it to the back side, oil it, season with salt and pepper, bread it, then place it supine with two tablespoons of oil on a heat-proof dish or baking sheet.
4. Take three large salted anchovies (or four if small), clean them of scales and spines, mince them, and put them on the fire with two tablespoons of oil to break them down, being careful they do not boil.
5. Spread this sauce on the fish on the upper part, that is on the belly, and cover it completely with breadcrumbs, sprinkling some rosemary leaves on top if desired.
6. Cook it between two fires and let it form a crust, but be careful it does not dry out too much; for this reason, sprinkle more oil on it, and before removing it, squeeze a large lemon half over it.
7. It should be enough for four or five people if served at table surrounded by croutons with caviar or anchovies.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Nocino (Walnut Liqueur)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Walnuts with hull, 30 number
- Alcohol, one and a half liters
- Powdered sugar, 750 grams
- Grated cinnamon, 2 grams
- Whole cloves, 10 number
- Water, 4 deciliters
- The rind of one garden lemon in small pieces

## Directions

1. Cut the walnuts into four quarters and place them in infusion with all the aforementioned ingredients in a demijohn or flask with a capacity of four or five liters
2. Close it well and keep it for forty days in a warm place, shaking the vessel from time to time
3. Strain it through cloth and then, to make it clear, pass it through cotton or paper, but taste it a few days before, because if it seems too strong to you, you can add a glass of water to it
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Offelle (Filled Pastries)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- shortcrust pastry from previous recipe
- marzipan from recipe No. 579
- fresh orange zest for flavoring (if candied orange is not available)

## Directions

1. Use the same shortcrust pastry indicated in the previous recipe
2. For the filling, instead of jam, use the marzipan described at No. 579
3. If you do not have candied orange, use fresh orange zest for flavoring, which is very pleasant
4. You may give these offelle a different shape to distinguish them from others
5. Use a mold to shape them, folding it in two to form a scalloped half-moon shape
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Offelle di Marmellata (Jam Pastries)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Rose apples, 500 grams
- Powdered sugar, 125 grams
- Candied fruit, 30 grams
- Ground cinnamon, two teaspoons
- Short crust pastry

## Directions

1. Cut the apples into four wedges, peel them and remove the core
2. Cut these wedges into the thinnest slices you can and place them in a pot with two glasses of water, breaking them up with a wooden spoon
3. These apples have hard flesh and need water to cook; if they become too dry while boiling, add more water
4. Wait until they are completely broken down before adding the sugar and then taste to check if the sweetness is right, as fruits in general can be more or less acidic depending on ripeness
5. Finally add the candied fruit cut into small pieces and the cinnamon
6. Use the short crust pastry number 589 in the quantity of recipe A, roll it out with a rolling pin to the thickness of a coin and cut it with a round fluted stamp as shown in the previous page; one disc below and one disc above, the latter rolled with a ridged rolling pin, and jam in the middle, moistening the edges so they stick
7. Glaze the offelle with egg yolk and send them to the oven, dusting them afterwards with powdered sugar
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Offelle di Marzapane (Marzipan Pastries)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Short crust pastry
- Marzipan
- Fresh orange for flavoring (if you don't have candied orange)

## Directions

1. Use the same short crust pastry indicated in the previous recipe and for filling, instead of jam, use the marzipan described in recipe 579 and, if you don't have candied orange, use fresh orange for flavoring which is very pleasant
2. You can give these offelle a different shape to distinguish them from the others
3. Use a stamp that you can fold in two forming a scalloped half moon
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Olive in Salamoia (Brined Olives)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Olives, 1 kilogramm
- Ash, 1 kilogramm
- Quicklime, 80 grams
- Salt, 80 grams
- Water for brine, 8 deciliters
- Wild fennel stems, several large pieces

## Directions

1. Mix the quicklime (in its powdered state after it has slaked and crumbled) with the ash, then add water to form a paste that is neither too thick nor too thin
2. Immerse the olives in this mixture, weighing them down so they remain completely covered, and leave them for twelve to fourteen hours until they become somewhat softened, checking them frequently by tasting
3. Remove the olives from the paste, wash them in plenty of water, and leave them in fresh water for four to five days until the water runs clear and the bitterness is lost, changing the water three times a day
4. Heat the eight deciliters of water with the salt and several pieces of large wild fennel stems, bring to a boil for a few minutes
5. Pour this brine, when cooled, over the olives in a glass jar or glazed earthenware vessel
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Orzata (Almond Milk)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sweet almonds with 10 or 12 bitter ones, 200 grams
- Water, 600 grams
- Fine white sugar, 800 grams
- Orange flower water, two spoonfuls

## Directions

1. Shell the almonds and pound them in the mortar, moistening them from time to time with the orange flower water.
2. When they are reduced to an impalpable paste, dissolve them with one third of the said water and strain the juice through a cloth, squeezing it well.
3. Return to the mortar the dry paste remaining in the cloth, grind it with the pestle; then dissolve it with another third of the water and strain the juice.
4. Repeat the same operation for the third time, put all the obtained liquid on the fire, and when it is very hot pour in the sugar, stir it and let it boil for about twenty minutes.
5. When it is cold, bottle it and keep it in a cool place.
6. Made in this way, you will see that the orzata does not ferment and you can keep it for a long time, though not as long as fruit syrups.
7. Moreover it comes of such substance that very little, dissolved in a glass of water, is sufficient to obtain an excellent and refreshing drink.
8. Made with melon seeds, it comes out even more delicate.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Osso Buco (Braised Veal Shanks)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal shank bones, one per person
- Onion
- Celery
- Carrot
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper
- Flour
- Water
- Tomato juice or conserve
- Lemon rind
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Put as many pieces of bone on the fire as there are people who will eat it, on top of a raw and chopped mixture of onion, celery, carrot and a piece of butter; season it with salt and pepper. When it has taken flavor add another small piece of butter dipped in flour to give it color and to bind the gravy and cook it through with water and tomato juice or conserve. Pass the gravy, degrease it and put back on the fire, give it flavor with lemon rind cut into small pieces, adding a pinch of chopped parsley before removing it from the fire.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pagnottelle Ripiene (Filled Bread Rolls)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- bread rolls the size of a common apple
- milk
- egg
- lard or oil
- ground meat filling made with chicken livers, chicken breasts, sweetbreads and similar meats
- meat broth
- flour
- truffles

## Directions

1. Scrape the crust lightly with a grater
2. Make a round opening in the middle of each roll the size of a 10-centesimi coin
3. Empty out the crumb leaving the walls somewhat thick
4. Wet them inside and out with boiling milk
5. When sufficiently soaked, close them with their round piece, which should also be soaked
6. Dip them in egg to gild them
7. Fry them in lard or oil, dropping them into the pan with the lid side down so it adheres
8. After cooking, detach the round piece with a knife point
9. Fill with a delicate and hot meat filling of pieces the size of chickpeas, made with chicken livers, chicken breasts, sweetbreads and similar meats drawn with meat broth and bound with a pinch of flour, seasoned with truffles
10. Close them again and serve
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pallottole di Mandorle (Almond Balls)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sweet almonds, 140 grams
- Powdered sugar, 140 grams
- Grated or powdered chocolate, about 40 grams
- Maraschino liqueur, 4 tablespoons

## Directions

1. Blanch the almonds, dry them in the sun or by the fire and pound them very finely with two tablespoons of the said sugar.
2. Pour them from the mortar into a vessel, add the remaining sugar and knead the mixture with the said liqueur.
3. Spread the chocolate on a work surface and form from the aforementioned mixture as many balls slightly larger than hazelnuts, of which you will obtain more than 30; roll them on the chocolate so they remain well covered, and you will be able to keep them for a long time.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pallottole di Patate Ripiene (Stuffed Potato Balls)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes, 300 grams
- Parmesan cheese, 2 heaping spoonfuls
- Eggs, 2
- Nutmeg flavoring
- Flour, as needed

## Directions

1. Boil the potatoes, peel them and pass them hot through a sieve over a layer of flour.
2. Make a hollow on top of the potato mound, salt them, give them the flavor of nutmeg and pour the eggs and Parmesan into it.
3. With as little flour as possible, form a soft and long paste that you will divide into 18 parts and for each one, with floured fingers, make a small hollow to fill with a meat forcemeat.
4. Pull the edges over it to cover it and, with floured hands, form round balls that you will fry in lard or oil, sending them to the table as a side dish to fried meat.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pallottole di Semolino (Semolina Balls)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Semolina, 120 grams
- Butter, 15 grams
- Potato flour, one heaping spoonful, equal to 25 grams
- Eggs, one whole and two yolks
- Sugar, one heaping teaspoon
- Lemon zest flavoring
- Milk, 4 deciliters
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cook the semolina well in the milk with the said sugar, adding the butter, flavoring and a pinch of salt when you remove it from the fire
2. When it is completely cold, pound it in a mortar with the eggs, first the yolks one at a time then the whole egg
3. Add the potato flour last, working the mixture very well with the pestle
4. Pour it onto a plate and drop it into a pan by spoonfuls to obtain balls somewhat larger than walnuts, which you will serve dusted with icing sugar when they have lost their strong heat
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Palombo Fritto (Fried Dogfish)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Dogfish
- Egg, lightly salted
- Breadcrumbs
- Parmesan cheese
- Garlic
- Parsley
- Salt and pepper
- Lemon

## Directions

1. Cut the dogfish into rounds not too thick and soak them in lightly salted egg for a few hours
2. Half an hour before frying, coat them in a mixture made of breadcrumbs, Parmesan, chopped garlic and parsley, salt and pepper (a small garlic clove is sufficient per 500 grams of fish)
3. Fry and serve surrounded with lemon slices
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Palombo in Umido (Dogfish in Braise)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Dogfish
- Garlic
- Parsley
- Onion
- Oil
- Salt and pepper
- Red or dry white wine
- Tomato juice or tomato paste

## Directions

1. Cut the dogfish into fairly large pieces
2. Make a battute with garlic, parsley and very little onion
3. Put it in a pan with oil and when it has fried sufficiently, place the dogfish in it and season with salt and pepper
4. Once browned, add some red or dry white wine and tomato juice or tomato paste to complete the cooking
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pan di Fegato (Liver Pâté)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal liver, 500 grams
- Butter, 70 grams
- Fresh bread crumb, 50 grams
- Grated Parmesan cheese, 20 grams
- Chicken livers, 4 pieces
- Marsala wine, 1 deciliter
- Meat gravy or broth, 6 tablespoons
- Eggs, 1 whole and 2 yolks
- 1 bay leaf
- Salt and pepper, to taste

## Directions

1. Slice the liver into thin slices and cut the chicken livers in two parts, and throw these two things in a pan with the bay leaf and half the butter and when they have absorbed it add the other half and season with salt and pepper.
2. Pour the Marsala and after 4 or 5 minutes at most on high heat, the liver must remain tender; remove it dry and together with the bay leaf pound it in a mortar.
3. In the sauce that remains in the pan, crumble the bread crumb and make a paste which you will also throw in the mortar, then pass everything through a sieve; then add the Parmesan and the eggs, diluting the mixture with the said gravy or broth.
4. Finally place it in a smooth mold with paper underneath, greased with butter, and set it in a bain-marie.
5. Unmold it warm and when it is cold cover it all with gelatin from recipe 3, inside a mold of greater circumference than the first.
6. It will be enough for twelve people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Panata (Romagna Easter Soup)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Bread from the day before, grated, not pounded, 130 grams
- Eggs, 4
- Parmigiano cheese, 50 grams
- Nutmeg, to taste
- Salt, a pinch

## Directions

1. Take a wide saucepan and form in it a mixture that is not too stiff with the aforementioned ingredients, adding breadcrumbs if needed.
2. Dissolve it with hot broth, but not boiling, and set aside some to add afterwards.
3. Cook it with coals all around, little or no fire underneath and with a spoon, as it comes to a boil, seek to gather it in the middle moving it away from the sides of the vessel without breaking it apart.
4. When you see it has set, pour it into the soup tureen and serve it.
5. If the panata turned out well you will see it all in clusters with its clear broth around it.
6. If you like it mixed with herbs or with peas cook these things separately and mix them into the mixture before dissolving it with broth.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pane Bolognese (Bolognese Bread)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Wheat flour, 500 grams
- Powdered sugar, 180 grams
- Butter, 180 grams
- Raisins (Zibibbo), 70 grams
- Chopped pine nuts, 50 grams
- Candied citron in small fillets, 30 grams
- Cream of tartar, 8 grams
- Baking soda, 4 grams
- Eggs, 2
- Milk, 1 deciliter

## Directions

1. Mix the sugar with the flour and make a mound on the work surface
2. In the well you make in it, place the butter, eggs, and milk (this lukewarm with the two powders in it), which you will already see beginning to ferment
3. Knead everything together and when the dough has become homogeneous, open it to add the pine nuts, candied citron, and raisins
4. Work it so that these things are spread evenly throughout to form two breads in a shuttle shape, barely more than a finger high
5. Brush them with egg yolk and bake immediately in the oven or in a country oven
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pane di lepre (Hare Bread)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Lean hare without bone, 250 grams
- Butter, 100 grams
- Flour, 50 grams
- Grated Parmesan, 30 grams
- Egg yolks, 6 numbers
- Milk, half a liter

## Directions

1. Make a finely chopped battutino with about 20 grams of ham and a small piece of onion, put it on the fire with half of the butter and the hare cut into small pieces, salting it
2. When the fat is almost consumed and before the meat browns, pour good broth to cook it
3. When cooked, pound it in a mortar basting it with its juice, then pass it through a sieve
4. With the indicated flour, the rest of the butter and the milk make a béchamel and when it is cool beat the egg yolks well and mix everything together
5. Put the mixture in a smooth mold with buttered paper on the bottom and cook it in a water bath
6. Serve it cold, surrounded and covered with gelatin; but since nowadays in meals one seeks much the beauty and elegance in dishes, and also some pleasant surprise, it would be better in this case
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pane di Sabbia (Sand Bread)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fresh butter, 185 grams
- Powdered sugar, 185 grams
- Rice flour, 125 grams
- Starch flour, 125 grams
- Potato flour, 60 grams
- Eggs, 4
- Juice of one quarter of a lemon
- Cognac, one tablespoon
- Baking soda, one teaspoon
- Vanilla flavoring

## Directions

1. Work the butter alone first, then add the egg yolks one by one, always stirring the spoon in the same direction
2. Then pour in the sugar, then the cognac and lemon juice
3. Then the flours and lastly the baking soda and whipped egg whites; but of these latter, pour in first two tablespoons to soften the mixture, and fold in the rest gently
4. Pour the mixture into a proportioned pan, greased with butter and dusted with powdered sugar and flour, and bake it in the oven or in a country oven at moderate heat. About one hour of baking should be sufficient
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pane di Spagna (Spanish Bread)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs, 6
- fine powdered sugar, 170 grams
- Hungarian flour or finest flour, 170 grams
- lemon zest, to taste

## Directions

1. Beat first the egg yolks with the sugar, then add the flour, dried in the oven or sun, and after working it for about half an hour pour in two tablespoons of the six beaten egg whites to soften the mixture, then the rest mixing gently.
2. Bake it in the oven.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Panettone Marietta (Marietta's Panettone)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- finest flour, 300 grams
- butter, 100 grams
- sugar, 80 grams
- sultana raisins, 80 grams
- eggs, 1 whole and 2 yolks
- salt, a pinch
- cream of tartar, 10 grams
- baking soda, 1 teaspoon or about 5 grams
- candied fruit in small pieces, 20 grams
- lemon zest
- milk, about 2 deciliters

## Directions

1. In winter soften the butter in a bain-marie and work it with the eggs; add the flour and milk little by little, then the rest except the raisins and powders which you will save for last; but, before pouring these in, work the mixture for at least half an hour and reduce it with milk to the proper consistency, that is, neither too liquid nor too stiff.
2. Pour it into a smooth mold taller than it is wide and with double walls so that as it rises it does not overflow and can take the form of a round loaf. Grease the walls with butter, dust it with powdered sugar mixed with flour and bake it in the oven. If it turns out well you will see that it rises greatly forming a cracked swelling on top.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pappardelle all'Aretina (Wide Ribbon Pasta Aretine-Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one domestic duck
- butter
- salt
- pepper
- ham
- onion
- celery
- carrot
- broth and water
- veal or beef spleen
- tomato
- nutmeg
- egg pasta dough
- duck liver
- parmesan cheese

## Directions

1. Put the duck in a casserole with a piece of butter, season it with salt and pepper and, when it has taken color, add a finely chopped battuto of ham, onion, celery and carrot.
2. Let it wilt under the duck, turning it often; then remove a good part of the fat as an indigestible thing, and finish cooking it with broth and water poured little by little, in such a quantity that you have sauce left to season the pappardelle.
3. Get a piece of veal or beef spleen, open it and scrape out the inner part with a knife to boil it under the duck when this is cooked and it will serve as an ingredient for the sauce to which it is good to add also tomato and nutmeg.
4. Make a sheet of egg pasta, thick like that of tagliatelle and with the fluted wheel cut strips wider than a finger.
5. Cook them briefly and season them with the said sauce, with the duck liver in pieces, parmesan and a little butter if needed.
6. These pappardelle serve as a first course and the duck as a second course.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pappardelle col Sugo di Coniglio (Wide Ribbon Pasta with Rabbit Sauce)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- rabbit
- butter
- oil
- rabbit liver
- cured meat (carnesecca)
- onion
- celery
- carrot
- parsley
- salt
- pepper
- water
- tomato sauce or preserve
- parmesan cheese
- pappardelle or strips of pasta

## Directions

1. After washing the rabbit, cut it into pieces larger than those for frying and put it on the fire in a casserole to release its water which you will then drain.
2. When well dried, throw in a piece of butter, a little oil and a battuto finely chopped and composed of the animal's liver, a piece of cured meat and all the aromatics, namely: onion, celery, carrot and parsley.
3. Season with salt and pepper.
4. Stir it often and when it is browned, wet it with water and tomato sauce or preserve, to finish cooking it, adding at the end another piece of butter.
5. Use the sauce to season with this and with parmesan a pasta of pappardelle or strips, and bring to the table for the second course the rabbit with some of its sauce.
6. If you do not want to season the pasta, the cured meat is not necessary in the battuto.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pappardelle colla Lepre I (Pappardelle with Hare)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Two fillets of hare, weighing altogether 180 to 200 grams, including the kidneys
- Butter, 50 grams
- Cured meat (carnesecca), 40 grams
- Half an onion of medium size
- Half a carrot
- A piece of celery one palm long
- Scent of nutmeg
- Parmesan, as needed
- One spoonful of flour
- Meat broth, 6 decilitres
- Pappardelle or strips of pasta
- Salt and pepper

## Directions

1. Peel the fillets of the membrane that wraps them and cut them into small dice
2. Make a battuto with the cured meat, onion, celery and carrot
3. Chop it very finely with the crescent blade and put it on the fire with one third of the said butter and with the hare meat, seasoning it with salt and pepper
4. When the meat is browned, sprinkle flour over it and shortly after wet it and finish cooking it with the said broth
5. Before using this sauce, add the rest of the butter and the nutmeg
6. Cook the pappardelle or strips in salted water, lift them out well dried and dress them on the platter, without putting them back on the fire, with parmesan and the aforesaid sauce
7. In the absence of fillets, use the hind legs
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pappardelle colla Lepre I (Wide Ribbon Pasta with Hare Sauce I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- two hare fillets (180 to 200 grams total, including kidneys)
- butter (50 grams)
- cured meat (40 grams)
- half a medium onion
- half a carrot
- a piece of celery about a palm long
- nutmeg
- parmesan cheese
- flour (one tablespoon)
- meat sauce (6 deciliters)
- pappardelle made from three-egg pasta or 500 to 600 grams of store-bought pasta strips

## Directions

1. Skin the hare fillets of the membrane that wraps them and cut them into small dice.
2. Make a battuto with the cured meat, onion, celery and carrot.
3. Chop it very fine with a crescent-shaped knife and put it on the fire with a third of the said butter and with the hare meat, seasoning it with salt and pepper.
4. When the meat is browned, sprinkle it with flour and continue the preparation as indicated.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pappardelle colla Lepre II (Pappardelle with Hare - Second Recipe)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 50 grams of ham, more fat than lean
- One quarter of an onion
- Celery
- Carrot
- Very little parsley
- 40 grams of butter
- Hare meat, same quantity as before
- Salt and pepper
- Broth
- Tomato juice or conserve
- 30 grams of butter
- One spoonful of flour
- 30 grams of butter
- Scent of nutmeg
- Parmesan
- Pappardelle or strips of pasta

## Directions

1. Make a battuto with 50 grams of ham, more fat than lean, a quarter of onion, celery, carrot and very little parsley
2. Put it on the fire with 40 grams of butter and when it has softened, throw in the pieces of meat whole and season them with salt and pepper
3. Brown the meat and then, to cook it, wet it little by little with broth and tomato juice or conserve, so that there remains abundant liquid
4. When the meat is cooked, lift it out dry and chop it not too finely with the crescent blade
5. Make a roux with 30 grams of butter and one spoonful of flour and when it has taken on a blonde color on the fire, pour into it the chopped meat and its broth, adding another 30 grams of butter and the scent of nutmeg
6. Dress the pasta with this sauce and parmesan
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Passatelli di Semolino (Semolina Pasta Strands)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Semolina of fine grain, 150 grams
- Grated Parmesan, 30 grams
- Milk, 6 deciliters
- Eggs, two whole and two yolks
- Salt, nutmeg flavor and lemon zest

## Directions

1. Cook the semolina in the milk, and if you see that it does not become firm enough, add another pinch
2. Salt it when it is cooked and wait for it to lose heat before adding the eggs and the rest
3. Place the mixture in a syringe with a round-hole stamp rather large, and push it into boiling broth, holding the syringe perpendicular and let it boil until the passatelli have solidified
4. This dose will be sufficient for six or seven people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Passato di Patate (Mashed Potatoes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes, large and floury, 500 grams
- Butter, 50 grams
- Good milk or cream, half a glass
- Salt, to taste

## Directions

1. Boil the potatoes
2. Peel them while still hot
3. Pass them through a sieve while hot
4. Put them in a pot over the fire with the butter, milk or cream and salt
5. Work them well with a spoon to refine them
6. Test if cooked by piercing with a pointed stick that should pass through freely
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Frolla (Short Crust Pastry) - Recipe A
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, 500 grams
- White sugar, 220 grams
- Butter, 180 grams
- Lard, 70 grams
- Whole eggs, 2 and 1 yolk

## Directions

1. Pound the sugar very fine (or use powdered sugar) and mix it with the flour
2. If the butter is firm, make it pasty by working it first with a wet hand on a work surface
3. Ensure the lard does not have a rancid taste
4. Make a paste of everything, handling it as little as possible so it does not burn, as cooks say; use a knife blade to help mix it
5. You may prepare this paste one day in advance; it improves when cooked because it becomes more crumbly over time
6. When using it for pastries, tarts, cakes, etc., first thin it with a smooth rolling pin and then work the top part with a ridged rolling pin for beauty, brushing it with egg yolk
7. If you have leftover small pieces, unite them with a drop of white wine or Marsala, which also makes the paste more crumbly
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Frolla (Short Crust Pastry) - Recipe B
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, 250 grams
- Butter, 125 grams
- White sugar, 110 grams
- Whole eggs, 1 and 1 yolk

## Directions

1. Pound the sugar very fine (or use powdered sugar) and mix it with the flour
2. If the butter is firm, make it pasty by working it first with a wet hand on a work surface
3. Make a paste of everything, handling it as little as possible so it does not burn, as cooks say; use a knife blade to help mix it
4. You may prepare this paste one day in advance; it improves when cooked because it becomes more crumbly over time
5. When using it for pastries, tarts, cakes, etc., first thin it with a smooth rolling pin and then work the top part with a ridged rolling pin for beauty, brushing it with egg yolk
6. If you have leftover small pieces, unite them with a drop of white wine or Marsala, which also makes the paste more crumbly
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Frolla (Short Crust Pastry) - Recipe C
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, 270 grams
- Sugar, 115 grams
- Butter, 90 grams
- Lard, 45 grams
- Egg yolks, 4
- Orange zest flavoring

## Directions

1. Pound the sugar very fine (or use powdered sugar) and mix it with the flour
2. If the butter is firm, make it pasty by working it first with a wet hand on a work surface
3. Make a paste of everything, handling it as little as possible so it does not burn, as cooks say; use a knife blade to help mix it
4. You may prepare this paste one day in advance; it improves when cooked because it becomes more crumbly over time
5. When using it for pastries, tarts, cakes, etc., first thin it with a smooth rolling pin and then work the top part with a ridged rolling pin for beauty, brushing it with egg yolk
6. If you have leftover small pieces, unite them with a drop of white wine or Marsala, which also makes the paste more crumbly
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Genovese (Genoese Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sugar, 200 grams
- Butter, 150 grams
- Potato flour, 170 grams
- Wheat flour, 110 grams
- Egg yolks, 12
- Egg whites, 7
- Lemon zest flavoring

## Directions

1. Work the egg yolks, butter, and sugar together very well in a bowl
2. Add the two flours and work for approximately half an hour
3. Pour the beaten egg whites into the mixture
4. Transfer the batter to a copper baking pan prepared as usual with a light coating of butter and flour
5. Keep it about the thickness of a finger, cut into almond shapes when baked, and dust with powdered sugar
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Maddalena (Maddalena Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sugar, 130 grams
- Fine flour, 80 grams
- Butter, 30 grams
- Egg yolks, 4
- Egg whites, 3
- A pinch of baking soda
- Lemon zest flavoring

## Directions

1. First work the egg yolks with the sugar, and when they have become whitish, add the flour and work again for more than a quarter hour
2. Add to the mixture the melted butter if it is winter, and last the beaten egg whites
3. Dry the flour by the fire or in the sun if it is summer
4. You can give this paste different forms, but always keep it thin and of little volume
5. It is customary to put it in worked molds, greased with butter and floured, or in a pan to the thickness of barely a finger, cutting it afterwards into almond shapes which you will dust with powdered sugar
6. You can also make it half a finger thick and stick the almonds together two by two with fruit preserves
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Margherita (Margherita Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Potato flour, 120 grams
- Powdered sugar, 120 grams
- Eggs, 4
- Juice of one lemon

## Directions

1. First beat very well the egg yolks with the sugar, add the flour and lemon juice and work all of it for more than half an hour
2. Finally beat the egg whites, unite them to the rest mixing with delicacy so as not to deflate the foam
3. Pour the mixture into a smooth and round mold, or into a proportioned baking pan, buttered and dusted with powdered sugar and flour, and place it immediately in the oven
4. Unmold it while cold and dust it with vanilla powdered sugar
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Matta (Simple Flour Paste)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. Mix flour with water and salt in proportion and form a dough that can be rolled out thin with a rolling pin
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta per Pasticci Diacci di Carne (Dough for Cold Meat Pies)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, 250 grams
- Butter, 70 grams
- Salt, a generous pinch
- Milk as needed to blend and bring to proper consistency

## Directions

1. It does not need to be worked excessively. Form it into a loaf and let it rest for about half an hour, wrapped in a damp cloth and dusted with flour. This amount should be sufficient for a pie even larger than the game pie described in recipe no. 370.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Sfoglia (Puff Pastry)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fine flour or Hungarian flour, 200 grams
- Butter, 150 grams
- Or alternatively: flour, 300 grams
- Butter, 200 grams
- Hot water (not boiling)
- Salt to taste
- One spoonful of aquavit
- Butter the size of a walnut

## Directions

1. In winter, mix the flour with hot water that is not boiling, salt to taste, one spoonful of aquavit and butter the size of a walnut, taken from the aforementioned 150 or 200 grams. Once you have formed a dough that is neither too firm nor too soft, work it extensively, at least half an hour, first with your hands, then by throwing it forcefully against the work surface. Shape it into a rectangular loaf, wrap it in a kitchen cloth and let it rest briefly.
2. In the meantime, work the butter, if it is firm, with a hand dampened in water on the work surface to make it smooth and uniformly tender throughout. Then shape it into a loaf like that of the flour and place it in a bowl of fresh water. When the dough has rested, remove the butter from the water, dry it with a cloth and dust it thoroughly with flour.
3. Roll out the dough with a rolling pin only as much as necessary to enclose the butter loaf. Place the butter in the center and fold the edges of the dough over it, joining them together with your fingers and ensuring it adheres to the butter in all parts so no air remains between them.
4. Now begin to roll it out first with your hands, then with the rolling pin, thinning it out the first time as much as you can, being careful that the butter does not burst through. If this happens, immediately sprinkle a bit of flour where the butter appears, and frequently dust flour on the work surface and rolling pin so that the dough slides and stretches beneath it.
5. After the first rolling, fold the dough in three, like three overlapping sheets, and roll it out again to a reasonable thickness. Repeat this operation six times in total, letting the dough rest for ten minutes from time to time. On the last, which would be the seventh, fold it in two and reduce it to the thickness needed, that is something less than one centimeter.
6. Except for this last folding, make sure to give the dough a rectangular shape each time you stretch it, three times longer than it is wide, and if air pockets appear, prick them with a needle.
7. A marble slab would be better than a common work surface as it is colder and smoother. In summer, ice is necessary, both to firm up the butter before using it and to stretch the dough better, which is achieved by passing ice over the dough when necessary, wrapped in a very tight kitchen cloth or, better, placed between two plates covered with ice.
8. With puff pastry, as you know, one makes vol-au-vent, small pastries with jam or preserves, and pies filled with marzipan. If you wish to use the pastries as an intermediate course, fill them with a delicate mixture of meat, liver and sweetbreads. But in all ways these pastries should be glazed with egg yolk on the surface but not on the edges to avoid preventing puffing. If they are for dessert, dust them while hot with powdered sugar.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasta Sfoglia a Metà (Half Puff Pastry)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Butter, half the weight of the flour plus an extra small piece
- See preceding recipe for other ingredients

## Directions

1. Proceed as in the preceding recipe, but use half the amount of butter relative to the flour weight, plus an additional small piece incorporated into the dough.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Paste alla Cacciatora (Pasta Hunter-Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- two teal ducks
- pasta (small shapes like nocette, paternostri, penne)
- celery
- carrot
- parsley stems
- salt
- water
- butter
- parmesan cheese

## Directions

1. Discard the head, feet, tail and intestines of the teal to boil them with a bunch garnished with celery, carrot and parsley stems in enough salted water to cook the pasta.
2. When cooked, bone them and chop them with a crescent-shaped knife together with the livers and gizzards emptied that you will have cooked with the teal.
3. Cook the pasta in the said broth, drain it well and season it in layers with this chopped meat, butter and parmesan in good measure.
4. This makes a tasty pasta and, what matters more, of not difficult digestion.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Paste con le Arzavole (Pasta with Teal)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one teal duck
- onion
- celery
- carrot
- ham
- butter
- salt
- pepper
- broth
- tomato sauce or preserve
- dried mushrooms
- spices or nutmeg
- flour
- parmesan cheese
- pasta

## Directions

1. Clean and prepare the teal, place it in a pot with a battuto of onion, a piece of celery, half a carrot, 40 grams of ham and a piece of butter; season with salt and pepper.
2. When browned, finish cooking it with good broth and a little tomato sauce or preserve.
3. Then bone it and chop it together with some pieces of dried mushrooms if you had them united to the teal while it was cooking.
4. Return to the fire this mixture with the aroma of spices or nutmeg and a piece of butter kneaded with flour to bind it, and with it and parmesan season 350 grams of pasta which can be macaroni, strips, penne or other similar shapes.
5. This quantity can be enough for five people if they are not big eaters.
6. If you add 50 grams of beef fillet to the teal you will have a more substantial sauce.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Paste di Farina Gialla I (Cornmeal Cookies I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Cornmeal, 200 grams
- Wheat flour, 150 grams
- Powdered sugar, 150 grams
- Butter, 100 grams
- Lard (rendered fat), 50 grams
- Anise, 10 grams
- Eggs, 1

## Directions

1. Mix together the two flours, sugar, and anise and knead with the butter, lard, and egg, making as much dough as you can, forming it into a loaf that you set aside
2. Mix the remaining dough with a little white wine and a little water and form another loaf, then combine the two loaves and work them as little as possible, ensuring the paste is rather soft
3. Roll it out with a rolling pin to the thickness of half a finger, dusting it with mixed flour so it does not stick to the work surface, and cut it with tin cutters into different shapes and sizes
4. Grease a baking pan with lard, dust it with flour, and place the cookies on it, brush them with egg, bake them in the oven, and dust them with powdered sugar
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Paste di Farina Gialla II (Cornmeal Cookies II)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Cornmeal, 200 grams
- Butter, 100 grams
- Powdered sugar, 80 grams
- Dried flowers of common elderflower, 10 grams
- Egg yolks, 2

## Directions

1. If the paste becomes too firm when kneading, soften it with a drop of water
2. Roll it out with a rolling pin to slightly more than the thickness of a shield and cut it into small discs, as these can also be served with tea, and to make them more attractive they can be streaked on the surface in the same manner with the prongs of a fork or with a grater
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pastella per Fritti di Carne (Batter for Fried Meat)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, three heaping teaspoons
- Oil, two teaspoons
- Eggs, 2
- Salt, a pinch
- Pepper, a pinch

## Directions

1. Mix three heaping teaspoons of flour with two teaspoons of oil, add two eggs, a pinch of salt and mix well. This mixture will take on the appearance of a flowing cream and will serve to coat fried brain, sweetbreads, pancreas, lamb testicles, head of milk-fed veal and similar items. These things, some more some less, according to their nature, blanch them all, including the brain and sweetbreads which firm up when boiled. Salt the water and add a pinch of salt and pepper when you remove them from the water. Cut the pancreas into fillets lengthwise. Keep the sweetbreads about half a finger long. If the sweetbreads are from lamb, leave them whole. Cut the brains into pieces the size of a walnut, and keep the heads at a somewhat larger size. Toss the pieces in the batter after flouring them and fry them in pure lard or oil. These white fried items are often served with liver or milk-fed veal cutlets. Slice the liver very thinly, beat the cutlets with the back of a knife or mince the meat with a chopper to reunite it afterward into an elegant shape. Season both with salt and pepper, place them in a bath of beaten egg, and after a few hours, before frying them, roll them in fine breadcrumbs, repeating the operation even twice if necessary. Always accompany these fried items with lemon wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pastella per le Fritture (Batter for Frying)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour, 100 grams
- Fine oil, one spoonful
- Aquavite, one spoonful
- Eggs, 1
- Salt to taste
- Iced water as needed

## Directions

1. Mix the flour with the egg yolk and the other ingredients, pouring in the water little by little to make a batter that is not too liquid. Work it well with a spoon to blend it thoroughly, and let it rest for several hours. When you are about to use it, add the beaten egg white. This batter can serve for many fried dishes and especially for those of fruit and vegetables.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcini di Marzapane (Marzipan Small Cakes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Short pastry dough (recipe C from no. 589)
- Sweet almonds with three bitter ones, blanched, 180 grams
- Sugar, 150 grams
- Butter, 25 grams
- Candied orange, 25 grams
- One egg yolk
- Several tablespoons of water

## Directions

1. Prepare short pastry dough according to recipe C from no. 589.
2. Make a marzipan according to the recipe from no. 579 with the given proportions.
3. Use brioches molds or slightly smaller ones, which would be better; butter them, line them with thin short pastry about the thickness of a coin, place the marzipan inside, fold the edges of the pastry over it, wet the rim with water, cover them with the same short pastry, brush the surface with egg, bake them in an oven or country oven and afterwards sprinkle them with powdered sugar.
4. With this amount you can make from 16 to 18.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcini di Pasta Beignet (Beignet Paste Small Cakes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Water, 150 grams
- Flour, 100 grams
- Butter, 10 grams
- Eggs, 3 and one yolk
- Salt, as needed

## Directions

1. When the water boils pour all the flour at once and, stirring immediately, add the butter and keep it on the fire for 10 minutes, continuing always to stir it.
2. It should turn out a hard paste which you will spread to the thickness of a finger and pound in a mortar together with one egg to soften it somewhat.
3. Having obtained this, put it in a bowl to work it with a wooden spoon, adding the other eggs one at a time, whipping the whites.
4. Do not tire of working it until it is reduced like an ointment; let it rest for a few hours, and then put it in spoonfuls (which will be about ten or twelve) in a buttered baking pan.
5. Whisk one egg yolk with a little white to make it more fluid, brush them and smooth them with a small brush (but this supplement is not necessary), then put them in a well-heated oven.
6. When they are cooked make a slit on one side with a small knife, or in the shape of a half-circle on the underside, to fill them with cream or fruit preserves, sprinkle them with powdered sugar and serve them.
7. I warn you that when you work pastes that must puff up, the wooden spoon instead of moving it in circles is better to move it from bottom to top.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcini di Pasta Beignet Coperti di Cioccolata (Beignet Pastries Covered with Chocolate)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Beignet paste (recipe N. 631)
- Cream, whipped cream, or fruit preserves for filling
- Chocolate, 120 grams
- Powdered sugar, 50 grams
- Water, 1 deciliter

## Directions

1. Prepare 20 to 23 small beignets following recipe N. 631.
2. Fill them with cream, whipped cream, or fruit preserves.
3. Whip the following mixture in the chocolate pot on the fire: chocolate, powdered sugar and water.
4. When well whipped, like chocolate served in a cup, pour it while boiling over the pastries placed one by one on a serving platter where they form a mound.
5. This is a dish best made the same day it is to be served, otherwise it hardens.
6. This quantity will suffice for six people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcini di Riso (Rice Small Cakes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Rice, 150 grams
- Sugar, 70 grams
- Butter, 30 grams
- Candied fruit, 30 grams
- Milk, 8 deciliters
- Eggs, 3
- Rum, 2 tablespoons
- Salt, a pinch

## Directions

1. Cook the rice very much, stirring it often with a wooden spoon so it does not stick.
2. At two-thirds of cooking pour in the sugar, butter, salt and candied fruit cut into small pieces.
3. When it is cooked and cooled add the rum, the egg yolks first and the whipped egg whites after.
4. Take brioches molds, butter them well, sprinkle them with breadcrumbs, fill them and bake them in a country oven.
5. They are better hot than cold.
6. With this amount you will make 12 or 14.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcini di Semolino (Semolina Small Cakes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Semolina, 180 grams
- Sugar, 100 grams
- Pine nuts, 50 grams
- Butter, 20 grams
- Milk, 8 deciliters
- Eggs, 4
- Salt, a pinch
- Lemon zest flavoring

## Directions

1. Cook the semolina in milk and when it begins to thicken pour in the pine nuts pounded in a mortar together with the sugar; then the butter and the rest, except the eggs, which you will save for last when the mixture is cooled.
2. For the rest proceed as with the rice small cakes from no. 630.
3. Before serving sprinkle them with powdered sugar.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio a Sorpresa (Surprise Pastry)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Milk, one liter
- Rice flour, 200 grams
- Sugar, 120 grams
- Butter, 20 grams
- Eggs, 6
- Salt, a pinch
- Vanilla flavoring

## Directions

1. Take a saucepan and gradually pour into it the eggs, sugar, flour and milk, mixing as you go so that no lumps form; but reserve some of the milk to add later if needed.
2. Place the saucepan on the fire and, stirring the spoon continuously as in making cream, cook the mixture adding, before removing it from heat, the butter, vanilla and salt.
3. Let it cool completely, then pour it into a metal plate or earthenware dish that can withstand the fire, arranged so that it forms a mound.
4. Cover it with shortcrust pastry (recipe 589, recipe B or recipe C), make some decorative work on it, brush it with egg yolk, bake it in the oven and serve it hot, dusted with powdered sugar.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio all'uso di Romagna (Romagna-Style Macaroni Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Macaroni, 350 grams
- Parmesan cheese, 170 grams
- Sweetbreads, 150 grams
- Butter, 60 grams
- Truffles, 70 grams
- Fat and lean prosciutto, 30 grams
- A handful of dried mushrooms
- Giblets of 3 or 4 chickens and their gizzards
- Chicken crests, bean-like organs and unborn eggs, if available
- Nutmeg flavoring
- Short crust pastry dough
- Egg yolk for gilding

## Directions

1. Parboil the macaroni in salted water until half-cooked, drain well and pass them through sauce No. 4, then let them absorb the sauce over very low heat until fully cooked.
2. Meanwhile prepare a béchamel sauce at half the dose of recipe No. 137 and cook the giblets with butter, salt and a pinch of pepper, moistening with sauce.
3. Cut the giblets and sweetbreads into pieces the size of a small walnut; after cooking, add the prosciutto in small strips, the truffles in thin slices, the mushrooms (first softened in warm water) and some nutmeg, mixing everything together.
4. Prepare the short crust pastry using the full dose of recipe No. 589, recipe A, flavoring it with lemon zest.
5. Grease a copper pie dish well with butter; drain the macaroni of excess sauce and spread a first layer, seasoning it with grated Parmesan, small pieces of butter scattered here and there and a few spoonfuls of béchamel and giblets; repeat this operation until all ingredients are used, filling the dish.
6. Roll out a sheet of short crust pastry one scudo thick, first with a smooth roller, then with a ridged one, and cover the macaroni up to the rim; then roll out two strips two fingers wide and form a cross over the top to reinforce the covering; wrap the edges with a band as wide as the rim of the dish and make ornaments with any remaining pastry, not forgetting to garnish the top with a nice bow.
7. Brush the entire surface with egg yolk, place the pie in the oven, and in its absence cook it in a country oven; finally serve it hot to those eager to enjoy a good meal.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di Carciofi e Piselli (Artichoke and Pea Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Artichokes, 12
- Shelled peas, 150 grams
- Butter, 50 grams
- Grated Parmesan cheese, 50 grams
- Meat broth, as needed
- Flour, 1 tablespoon
- Shortcrust pastry dough (recipe below)
- Egg yolk

## Directions

1. Clean the artichokes of all hard inedible leaves, divide them in half and remove the fuzzy center if present.
2. Give the artichokes and peas a brief half-cooking of a few minutes in salted water, then transfer them to fresh water, lift them out, and dry them well; divide the artichokes in half again.
3. Place both the artichokes and peas on the fire with 40 grams of the mentioned butter, season with salt and pepper, and finish cooking them with meat broth.
4. With the remaining 10 grams of butter, 1 tablespoon of flour and the aforesaid broth, make a kind of béchamel to bind the mixture.
5. Place the mixture in a fireproof dish and season it in layers with this béchamel and Parmesan cheese.
6. Cover it with the shortcrust pastry noted below; brush it with egg yolk, bake the pie in a country oven and serve it hot because it loses much if left to cool.
7. This quantity can serve seven or eight people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di Carciofi e Piselli (Artichoke and Pea Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- artichokes, 12
- shelled peas, 150 grams
- butter, 50 grams
- grated parmesan cheese, 50 grams
- meat sauce, as needed

## Directions

1. Clean the artichokes of all hard inedible leaves, divide them in two and remove the hair from the center if they have any.
2. Give them and the peas half cooking of a few minutes in salted water, then throw them in fresh water, remove them, dry them well, and divide the artichokes again into two parts.
3. Place both them and the peas on the fire with 40 grams of the said butter, season with salt.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di Carne (Meat Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Lean veal, 200 grams
- Lean pork, 100 grams
- Butter, 60 grams
- Cooked prosciutto cut thick, 60 grams
- Salted tongue cut thick, 50 grams
- Bread marrow, 50 grams
- Chicken breast, 1
- Chicken liver, 1
- A lark or similar bird, 1
- Truffle, 1
- Marsala, one deciliter

## Directions

1. Put on the fire with the said butter and season with salt and pepper the veal, pork, and the bird (from which you will remove the beak and feet).
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di Fegato (Liver Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Liver mixture from recipe 374
- Black truffles, 30 grams, cut into wedges
- Pastry from recipe 372

## Directions

1. Use the mixture from recipe 374, adding only 30 grams of black truffles cut into wedges and bringing them to a boil in the Marsala before scattering them in the pie.
2. Cover it with the pastry from recipe 372, cook it in the oven or in a country oven, and serve it cold.
3. It will be enough for twelve people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di lepre (Hare Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Half hare without head and paws, one kilogram
- Lean veal milk-fed, 230 grams
- Butter, 90 grams
- Salted tongue, 80 grams
- Ham fat, 80 grams
- Fat and lean ham, cut thick half a finger, 50 grams
- Ham, cut fine, 30 grams
- Black truffles, 60 grams
- Flour for the béchamel, 30 grams
- Marsala, 3 deciliters
- Eggs, 2 numbers
- Milk, half a glass
- Broth, as needed

## Directions

1. After washing and drying the hare, remove 80 grams of lean meat from the fillet and set aside, then separate all meat from bones, break the bones and set aside, cut the meat into pieces and with the 80 grams of lean meat left whole, put it in infusion with about two-thirds of the marsala and with the following aromatics cut roughly: a quarter of a large onion, half a carrot, one celery rib about a palm long, several parsley stems and two bay leaves, season with salt and pepper, turn everything well and let it rest for several hours
2. Meanwhile clean the veal of membranes, chop it with a knife and pound it in a mortar as fine as possible
3. Drain the hare meat from the marsala and with all the bones, the indicated aromatics, the ham fat cut into small pieces and 30 grams of the butter, put it in a covered pot and at high heat let it brown well, stirring often with a spoon and basting, when dry, with marsala, also using the remaining infusion, and with broth until completely cooked
4. Then separate the meat from the bones again and set aside the 80 grams of lean for forming with this, with 50 grams of ham and with the tongue, so many fillets thicker than half a finger
5. First pound all the hare meat in a mortar, basting it from time to time to make it more pasty but not too liquid, with the rest of the marsala and with broth and pass it; then pound the bones and ensure that as much as possible of these passes through, using a wire sieve for this purpose
6. Make a béchamel with 30 grams of the butter, the indicated flour and milk and, when cooked, pour into the same pot all the passed meat, both that of the hare and the raw veal, add the two eggs, mix well and taste the mixture to see if it is seasoned correctly, adding salt and the rest of the butter if needed
7. Now line the pie with the pasta described below and to fill it follow recipe 370, cut the truffles into chunks the size of hazelnuts and thus raw and with all the fillets described arrange them in three layers alternated with the well pressed mixture so they are spread regularly and make a fine show when the pie is cut, finally spread the 30 grams of ham in rather thin slices on top and cover it
8. You can cover it with puff pastry at half, like that of recipe 155, or with the following: flour 250 grams, butter 80 grams, wine spirit 2 teaspoons, sugar 2 teaspoons, egg yolks 2 numbers, the juice of one lemon wedge, salt 5 grams, cold water if needed
9. With this method, except for some variations as needed, you can make several other game pies, such as wild boar, fallow deer and roe deer, this should be enough for a meal even for twenty people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio di Magro (Lean Pastry Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- fish weighing 300 to 350 grams
- rice 200 grams
- fresh mushrooms 150 grams
- green peas 300 grams
- toasted pine nuts 50 grams
- butter as needed
- Parmesan cheese as needed
- artichokes 6 pieces
- eggs 2
- onion
- celery
- carrot
- parsley
- tomato juice or tomato paste
- salt
- pepper
- flour 400 grams
- white wine or Marsala two spoonfuls

## Directions

1. Cook the rice with 40 grams of butter and a quarter of chopped onion, salt it, and when cooked with the necessary water bind it with the said eggs and 30 grams of Parmesan. Make a soffritto with onion, butter, celery, carrot and parsley and in it cook the mushrooms cut into slices, the peas, and the artichokes cut into wedges and half-cooked. Finish cooking these things with a few spoonfuls of hot water and season them with salt, pepper and 50 grams of grated Parmesan when you have removed them from the fire. Cook the fish, which can be mullet, a type of fish, or even cut fish, in a soffritto of oil, garlic, parsley, tomato juice or tomato paste and season it with salt and pepper. Remove the fish, pass its sauce and in this dissolve the pine nuts that you have first toasted and crushed. Remove from the fish the head, backbone and small bones, cut it into pieces, return it to its sauce and add everything except the rice. Now that the elements of the pie are all ready, make the pastry to enclose it with the following amounts: flour 400 grams, butter 80 grams, eggs 2, white wine or Marsala two spoonfuls, a pinch of salt. Take any mold, grease it with butter and line it with the said pastry rolled thin; then fill it by first pouring in half the rice, then all the filling and on top of the filling the rest of the rice, covering it at the mouth with the same pastry. Cook it in the oven, unmold it and serve it lukewarm or cold. Made in the indicated amounts it will be enough for twelve people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pasticcio Freddo di Cacciagione (Cold Game Pie)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One partridge (gray partridge or red partridge), somewhat aged
- Chicken livers, 3
- Egg yolk, 1
- Bay leaves, 2
- Marsala, two fingers of a common glass
- Black truffles, 50 grams
- Salted tongue, 50 grams
- Fat and lean prosciutto, 30 grams
- Butter, 30 grams
- Bread marrow, a fistful
- Small battuto of onion, carrot, and celery
- Broth, a little
- Pastry dough (Recipe No. 372)

## Directions

1. Clean the partridge, wash it, and put it on the fire with the battuto, butter, prosciutto cut into slices, whole bay leaves, and season with salt and pepper.
2. When the onion takes on color, wet it with marsala poured little by little, and if it is not enough to bring the partridge to half-cooked, use broth.
3. Remove the partridge from the fire, take the breast and make eight fillets that you will set aside.
4. Cut the rest into small pieces to cook completely, together with the chicken livers, including that of the partridge.
5. When cooked, remove it dry and put it in a mortar, discarding the bay leaves.
6. Into the remaining sauce, throw the bread marrow and with a little broth, stirring, make a paste that you will pour also into the mortar, as well as the scrapings of the truffles, and pound everything well and fine to pass through a sieve.
7. In this paste, blend the egg yolk and work it well with a spoon to make it creamy.
8. Make the pastry to cover it using recipe No. 372.
9. Take one of those special molds for these pies, which are made in the shape of a boat or round, fluted, of white iron, with a hinge that opens.
10. Grease it with butter and, having rolled out the pastry thin slightly more than a coin, line it with the same and make the bottom which you will place on a copper baking sheet also greased with butter.
11. First throw into the bottom part of the mixture and arrange on top part of the fillets (breast of the partridge and tongue) and some pieces of truffles as big as hazelnuts and raw; then other mixture interspersed with truffles and fillets and so on if the pie were voluminous.
12. Press it well so that it comes together unified and compact and make it a cover of the same pastry with some ornament, leaving in the middle a hole so that the vapor escapes.
13. Gild it on the outside and bake it in the oven or in a country oven, and when you take it out cover the hole with a knot of the same pastry, made to measure and baked separately.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pastine o Capellini sul Brodo di Ombrina (Small Pasta or Capellini in Umbrina Broth)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Umbrina fish
- Water

## Directions

1. Boil the umbrina naturally, without any flavoring herbs.
2. This provides a broth that, almost like meat broth, is suitable for a light soup during lean days.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pastine o Capellini sul Brodo di Ombrina (Thin Pasta or Capellini in Ombrina Fish Broth)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Ombrina (a type of fish), 500 grams
- Pastine or capellini, 120 grams
- Butter, 30 grams
- Water, 1 liter
- Tomato juice
- Parmesan cheese

## Directions

1. Put the ombrina in cold water over the fire and salt it
2. When cooked, pass the broth through a strainer and cook the pasta in it
3. Season the pasta with tomato juice to hide the fish smell
4. Pour the pasta into a tureen where you have placed a piece of butter
5. Serve with parmesan cheese on the side as is customary for meat-based pasta dishes
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pastine pel Thè (Tea Pastries)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Hungarian or finest flour, 440 grams
- Potato flour, 160 grams
- Powdered sugar, 160 grams
- Butter, 160 grams
- Two egg whites
- Tepid milk, as needed

## Directions

1. Form a mound on the work surface with the two flours and sugar mixed together
2. Make a hole in the middle, place the egg whites and butter in pieces, and with the blade of a knife first and with your hands after, using the milk, knead it and work it for about half an hour to obtain a rather soft paste
3. Roll it out with a rolling pin into a sheet the thickness of a coin, cut it into round disks like those of N. 7, perforate them with the tines of a fork and bake them in the oven or in a country oven in a butter-greased pan
4. With only half the dose of the recipe, you will obtain quite a lot
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Patate alla sautè (Potatoes Sautéed)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- patate (potatoes)
- burro (butter)
- sale (salt)
- pepe (pepper)
- olio (oil)
- farina (flour)

## Directions

1. This means, in good Italian, potatoes browned in butter.
2. Peel raw potatoes and cut them into thin slices which you will put on the fire in a baking pan with butter, seasoning with salt and pepper.
3. It is very suitable to place them under the steak when it is sent to the table.
4. They can also be fried in a pan with oil in the following manner. If they are new potatoes, there is no need to peel them; just rub them with a rough cloth.
5. Cut them into very thin slices and leave them in fresh water for about an hour; then dry them well between the folds of a cloth and flour them.
6. Be careful not to roast them too much and salt them after cooking.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Patate Tartufate (Potatoes with Truffles)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes, partially boiled
- Truffles, sliced thin
- Grated Parmesan cheese
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper
- Broth or meat sauce
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. Slice partially boiled potatoes thinly
2. Layer potatoes in a small baking dish alternating with thin slices of truffle and grated Parmesan
3. Add small pieces of butter, salt and pepper
4. When they begin to sizzle, moisten with broth or meat sauce
5. Before removing from heat, squeeze a little lemon juice over them
6. Serve hot
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Patate tartufate (Truffled Potatoes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- patate (potatoes)
- tartufi (truffles)

## Directions

1. Cut into thin slices some potatoes already half boiled and place them in layers in a small baking pan, interspersed with truffles, also in slices.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Peperoni Affogati (Stewed Peppers)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Bell peppers
- Oil
- Butter
- Garlic
- Tomato juice
- Salt

## Directions

1. Briefly scald the peppers in a pan with a little oil so they can be peeled.
2. After peeling, put a finely chopped garlic clove in a pan with oil and butter and when it is browned, add the peppers.
3. Salt them, let them draw out flavor briefly, and add tomato juice.
4. Do not cook them too much as they would lose their piquancy, which is what gives them grace.
5. Serve them with boiled meat.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pere in Composta I (Pears in Compote I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Pears, 600 grams
- Fine powdered sugar, 120 grams
- Water, two glasses
- Half a lemon

## Directions

1. If they are small pears, leave them whole with their stems; if they are large, cut them into wedges: as you peel them, drop them into the aforementioned water in which you have squeezed the half lemon
2. This serves to preserve the whiteness of the fruit
3. Boil them in the same water passed through a strainer, pour in the sugar when it begins to boil and for the rest follow the procedure as for the apricots
4. Serve them cold
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pere in Composta II (Pears in Compote II)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Slightly underripe small pears, 600 grams
- Sugar, 120 grams
- Water, two glasses
- Half a lemon

## Directions

1. Squeeze the lemon into the water and set it aside
2. Place the pears on the fire covered with water and boil them for four or five minutes; then throw them into fresh water, peel them, cut half of their stem off, and as you peel them drop them into the water prepared with lemon
3. Having done this, take the same water, pass it through a strainer, put it on the fire and when it begins to boil pour in the sugar and let it boil somewhat
4. Then throw in the pears to cook them, but in such a way that they do not fall apart
5. Remove them dry, place them in a compote dish, reduce the syrup and pour it over the pears, passing it through the strainer once more
6. If they are very large pears, cut them into wedges after they have undergone the first boiling
7. Serve them cold
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Perine di Riso (Rice Pears)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Rice, 100 grams
- Milk, half a liter
- Butter, slightly more than a walnut
- Parmesan cheese, a good pinch
- Eggs, 1
- Chicken livers, 2
- Lamb sweetbreads, 2
- Nutmeg flavoring
- Prosciutto pieces
- Truffle or mushroom pieces

## Directions

1. Cook the rice well in the milk adding the butter and when it is cooked, salt it and wait for it to lose the strong heat to beat in the egg and add the Parmesan.
2. Cook two chicken livers and two lamb sweetbreads, making a delicate sauce, and give it the flavor of nutmeg; cut it into pieces smaller than a hazelnut and add pieces of prosciutto, truffles or mushrooms that give it much grace.
3. To give this filled rice the shape of pears have a small tin mold made and fill it half with rice, place two or three pieces of the sauce described and finish filling it with other rice.
4. Form the pear, and to extract it blow from the small tube, repeating the operation until you have used all the mixture.
5. To fry these pears you must gild them with eggs and breadcrumbs.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce a Taglio in Umido (Fish Stew)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fish fillet (tuna, ombrina, dentice, or ragno), about 600 grams
- Oil
- Onion
- Celery
- Parsley
- Salt
- Pepper
- Whole clove
- Tomato sauce or tomato conserve

## Directions

1. Remove the scales, wash and dry the fish, dust it with flour, and brown it slightly with a little oil.
2. Remove it, discard the remaining oil, and clean the pot.
3. Make a finely minced mixture with half a medium onion, a palm-length piece of white celery, and a good pinch of parsley.
4. Put it on the fire with sufficient oil and season with salt, pepper, and one whole clove.
5. When it has taken color, stop it with plenty of tomato sauce or conserve dissolved in water.
6. Let it boil a little and then place the fish in it to finish cooking, turning it often.
7. Serve it with plenty of its thick sauce so the fish is well-covered in it.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce al Piatto (Fish on a Plate)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- various small fish or fish cut into thin slices
- garlic
- parsley
- salt
- pepper
- oil
- lemon juice
- good white wine

## Directions

1. Clean, wash and dry the fish.
2. Place it in a metal or porcelain vessel that can withstand fire and season it with a mixture of minced garlic and parsley, salt and pepper, oil, lemon juice and good white wine.
3. Place half of the mixture with a little oil at the bottom, lay the fish over it, and then, pouring more oil and the rest of the ingredients, make sure the fish swims in it.
4. Cook it with fire below and above; if the dish is porcelain, place it on hot ashes.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce col Pangrattato (Fish with Breadcrumbs)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Leftover boiled fine fish
- Béchamel sauce
- Salt
- Grated Parmesan
- Truffles (or dried mushrooms if truffles are unavailable)
- Butter
- Breadcrumbs

## Directions

1. Cut the fish into small pieces and clean it well of bones and spines.
2. Put it in the béchamel sauce and season it with sufficient salt, grated Parmesan, and finely cut truffles, or if lacking these, use a pinch of rehydrated dried mushrooms.
3. Take a heat-proof dish, butter it, and sprinkle it with breadcrumbs.
4. Pour the mixture into it and cover it with a thin layer of breadcrumbs as well.
5. Finally, place a small piece of butter on top in the center, brown it in the oven, and serve it hot.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce di Maiale Arrosto (Roasted Pork Loin)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- pork loin (the elongated muscle at the sides of the spine)
- croutons
- sage
- oil

## Directions

1. The pork loin is that elongated muscle positioned at the sides of the spine, which in Florence is called loin of pork.
2. Cut it into small pieces and thread it onto a spit, interspersing it with croutons and sage as is done with birds, and oil it as you would those.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene
