title: Pesce Lesso (Boiled Fish)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fish
- Water
- Salt
- Onion
- Cloves
- Celery
- Carrot
- Parsley
- Lemon
- Beets (cooked)
- Potatoes (boiled)
- Hard-boiled eggs

## Directions

1. Put the necessary amount of water (not too much) on the fire and salt it.
2. Before putting in the fish, boil for about a quarter hour with the following aromatics: a quarter or half onion (depending on the quantity of fish), studded with two cloves, pieces of celery and carrot, parsley, and two or three lemon slices.
3. Alternatively, put it on the fire with cold water and the indicated aromatics, and after cooking, leave it hot in its broth until the time to serve it.
4. Rub the fish all over with lemon slices while still raw so the skin remains more intact.
5. The fish is done when the eyes pop out, the skin detaches when touched, and the fish becomes tender from boiling.
6. Serve it hot at the table, not completely dry from the water it was cooked in.
7. If desired, cover it with fresh parsley and arrange it in the center of a mixed side dish of beets (cooked in water if small, or in the oven if large) and boiled potatoes, both cut into very thin slices so they take on the seasoning better, and add a few hard-boiled egg wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce Marinato (Marinated Fish)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- sole or large eel
- oil
- salt
- pepper
- vinegar
- sapa (or sugar as substitute)
- whole sage leaves
- whole pine nuts
- raisins
- garlic cloves
- candied fruit, in small pieces

## Directions

1. If using sole, fry them first in oil and salt them; if using eel, cut it into pieces about half a finger long and, without skinning them, cook them on a grill or on a spit.
2. When they have rendered their fat, season them with salt and pepper.
3. In a pot, pour in proportion to the fish: vinegar, sapa (which goes with it like cheese on macaroni), whole sage leaves, whole pine nuts, raisins, a few cloves of garlic cut in half lengthwise and candied fruit in small pieces.
4. If you lack sapa, substitute with sugar and taste to correct the flavor of the vinegar, if it is too strong.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce Marinato (Marinated Fish)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sole or large eel
- Oil
- Vinegar
- Sapa (grape must reduction)
- Sage leaves
- Pine nuts
- Raisins
- Garlic
- Candied fruit
- Sugar (if sapa is unavailable)
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. If using sole, fry it first in oil and salt it. If using eel, cut it into pieces about half a finger long and cook them on a grill or spit without skinning them.
2. When the fish has rendered its fat, season with salt and pepper.
3. In a pot, pour vinegar, sapa (in proportion to the amount of fish), whole sage leaves, whole pine nuts, raisins, a few garlic cloves cut in half lengthwise, and candied fruit in small pieces.
4. If sapa is not available, substitute with sugar and taste to correct the flavor if the vinegar is too strong.
5. Bring this mixture to a boil and pour it over the fish that you have arranged in an earthenware dish, spreading it so the liquid covers it from all sides.
6. Bring it to a boil again with the fish inside, then cover the dish and set it aside.
7. When serving at table, take the quantity needed with some of its sauce, including some of the ingredients in it.
8. If over time the fish dries out, refresh it with a little more of the marinade.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce Marinato (Marinated Fish) - Alternative Preparation
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sole or eel
- Oil
- Vinegar
- Sapa
- Sage leaves
- Pine nuts
- Raisins
- Garlic
- Candied fruit
- Sugar

## Directions

1. Fry sole in oil and salt, or cut eel into half-finger-length pieces and cook on grill or spit without skinning.
2. Season with salt and pepper when fat is rendered.
3. In a pot, combine vinegar, sapa, whole sage leaves, whole pine nuts, raisins, garlic cloves cut in half, and candied fruit pieces in proportion to the fish.
4. Substitute sugar for sapa if needed and adjust vinegar strength to taste.
5. Bring to boil and pour over fish arranged in earthenware dish with liquid covering all sides.
6. Bring to boil again with fish inside, then cover and set aside.
7. When serving, take needed quantity with sauce and ingredients.
8. Refresh with additional marinade if fish dries out over time.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesce Squadro in Umido (Squatina Stew)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Pesce squadro (squatina), about 600 grams
- Parsley
- Carrot
- Celery
- Garlic
- Onion
- Oil
- Tomato sauce or tomato conserve
- Water
- Salt
- Pepper
- Butter
- Flour

## Directions

1. Make a finely minced mixture with a good pinch of parsley, half a carrot, a piece of celery, half a garlic clove, and onion the size of a large walnut (if the fish is about 600 grams).
2. Put the mixture on the fire with proportional oil and when it is browned, stop it with tomato sauce or conserve dissolved in half a glass of water.
3. Season with salt and pepper and place the piece of fish on top, preferably from the tail part, which is very thick.
4. Cook it slowly, and when it has reached two-thirds of cooking, add a small piece of butter well coated with flour to bind the sauce and give it a more delicate taste.
5. Finish cooking it.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesche in Ghiaccio (Peaches in Ice)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Ripe and sound peaches
- White powdered sugar
- Lemon
- Ice
- Salt

## Directions

1. Take ripe and sound peaches, throw them two at a time into boiling water for one minute
2. Remove them from the water and peel without touching the flesh
3. Roll them thoroughly in white powdered sugar and place them in a bowl or decent deep vase
4. Take as many small squares of sugar as there are peaches, rub them on the skin of a ripe garden lemon until each square is impregnated with the lemon's essence, and hide them among the peaches
5. Let them rest thus arranged for at least two hours
6. Before bringing the vase to the table, keep everything closed and covered in a lot of ice for two or three hours
7. Serve cold
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesche nello Spirito (Quince in Spirit)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Quince, 1 kilogram, not too ripe
- White sugar, 440 grams
- Water, 1 liter
- Whole cinnamon, one piece the length of a finger
- Some cloves
- Spirit of wine as needed

## Directions

1. Rub the quince with a cloth to remove the down and prick them in five or six places with a small stick
2. Boil the sugar in the water in an open pan for twenty minutes
3. Throw in the whole quince, stirring often if the syrup does not cover them, and when they have boiled for five minutes from when they return to a boil, remove them and let them dry
4. When the quince and syrup are cold, or better the next day, place them in a glass vase or new glazed earthenware vase, pour the syrup over them and enough spirit of wine or cognac to submerge them in the right measure
5. Add the indicated aromatics and ensure they always remain covered by the liquid, pouring more as needed
6. Keep the vase sealed hermetically and do not begin eating them before a month has passed
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pesche ripiene (Stuffed Peaches)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Large freestone peaches, not very ripe, 6
- Small ladyfinger biscuits, 4
- Powdered sugar, 80 grams
- Sweet almonds with three peach kernels, 50 grams
- Candied citron or orange, 10 grams
- Good white wine, just under half a glass

## Directions

1. Divide the peaches in two parts and remove the pits, enlarging the holes where they were with the tip of a knife
2. Unite the removed pulp with the peeled almonds, which you will pound very finely in a mortar with 50 grams of the sugar
3. To this mixture unite the ladyfinger biscuits made into crumbs, and lastly the candied fruit cut into very small dice
4. Fill and top the holes of the twelve peach halves with this filling, then place them evenly with the filling upward in a copper baking dish
5. Pour into it the wine and the remaining 30 grams of sugar and cook them between two fires to serve them hot or cooled as you prefer with their juice around them
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petonciani Fritti (Fried Eggplants)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggplants
- salt
- flour
- oil for frying

## Directions

1. Peel them, cut them into rather large chunks, salt them and let them sit for a few hours.
2. Dry them from the moisture they have released, flour them and fry them in oil.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petonciani in Gratella (Eggplants on the Grill)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggplants
- salt
- pepper
- oil

## Directions

1. Cut them in half lengthwise without peeling them, make cross-hatched incisions on the white part, season them with salt, pepper and oil, place them on the grill from the skin side; then cover them with a lid or iron pan and cook them between two fires, so they do not need to be turned; halfway through cooking give them another coating of oil.
2. They will be cooked when the flesh has become soft.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petonciani in Umido (Eggplants Stewed)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggplants
- butter
- tomato sauce (N. 125)

## Directions

1. Peel them, cut them into chunks and put them on the fire with a little butter.
2. When they have absorbed the butter, finish cooking them with tomato sauce N. 125.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petti di Pollo alla Sautè (Chicken Breasts Sautéed)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- chicken breasts
- salt
- pepper
- eggs, beaten
- fine breadcrumbs
- butter
- lemon juice
- optional: truffles or dried mushrooms, oil, grated Parmesan cheese, meat sauce or broth, tomato sauce or conserve

## Directions

1. Cut the breasts into slices as thin as possible, give them the best shape that will be possible and from the small pieces you get when cleaning the breastbone well, form a whole piece by joining them together and pressing them flat.
2. Season them with salt and pepper and put them to soak in beaten eggs.
3. After a few hours, pass them through fine breadcrumbs and cook them with butter in a sauté pan or in a baking dish.
4. If you like them plain, lemon juice is enough; if you want them with truffles, you can treat them as cutlets, or in the following manner:
5. Take a metal small pan, pour into it enough oil to just cover the bottom, spread a layer of truffle slices, sprinkling over it very little grated Parmesan and a pinch of breadcrumbs.
6. Repeat the same operation three or four times, according to the quantity, and finally season with oil, salt, pepper and a few small pieces of butter, all in small amounts so as not to be nauseating.
7. Put the pan on the fire and when it has come to a boil, water it with a ladle of meat sauce or broth and a little lemon juice.
8. Remove this sauce quickly from the fire and pour it over the breasts already browned in the aforementioned manner.
9. If you do not have truffles, use softened dried mushrooms chopped coarsely, and if lemon juice is lacking, have recourse to tomato sauce or conserve.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petti di Pollo alla Scarlatta (Chicken Breasts Scarlet)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Chicken or large hen breast
- Butter, 20 grams
- Flour, 40 grams
- Milk, 2 deciliters
- Salted tongue, 50 grams
- Eggs, 1
- Breadcrumbs
- Salt and pepper
- Lemon wedges

## Directions

1. From a capon breast or a large hen you will be able to get six thin slices, which at a meal will be enough for four or five people.
2. Cook them with butter and season with salt and pepper.
3. Make a béchamel with butter, flour and milk.
4. When it is cooked add 50 grams of finely chopped salted tongue with the lunette, and when it has cooled, spread the chicken breasts with it on all sides.
5. Dip them then in beaten egg, coat with breadcrumbs and brown them in butter or lard in a sauté pan, and serve them with lemon wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petto di vitella di latte in forno (Milk-Fed Veal Breast in the Oven)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- milk-fed veal breast with bones, weighing 600 to 700 grams
- prosciutto, lean, finely sliced, 50 to 60 grams
- lard (strutto)
- salt
- potatoes

## Directions

1. For a family dish, leave the piece as it is with all its bones.
2. If it does not exceed 600 to 700 grams, you can cook it in a country oven.
3. Lard it with finely sliced lean prosciutto.
4. Tie it so it stays together.
5. Coat it all abundantly with lard (strutto) and salt it.
6. Place it in a baking pan and about ten minutes before removing it from the fire, add potatoes which in that fat become very good.
7. Instead of lard you can use butter and oil, and instead of prosciutto salt it generously.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petto di Vitella di Latte Ripieno (Stuffed Milk Veal Breast)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Milk veal breast in one piece, 500 grams
- Lean milk veal without bone, 170 grams
- Fat and lean prosciutto, 40 grams
- Bologna mortadella, 40 grams
- Parmesan cheese, 15 grams
- One egg
- Just one quarter of a garlic clove and 4 or 5 parsley leaves

## Directions

1. Make a mixture as follows: clean the lean veal of tendons and pellicles if any, and mince it very finely with a piece of prosciutto fat
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Petto di Vitella di Latte Ripieno (Stuffed Milk-Fed Veal Breast)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Milk-fed veal breast in one piece, 500 grams
- Lean milk-fed veal without bone, 170 grams
- Fat and lean prosciutto, 40 grams
- Bologna mortadella, 40 grams
- Parmesan cheese, 15 grams
- Eggs, 1
- Just under a quarter of a garlic clove and 4 or 5 parsley leaves

## Directions

1. Make a mixture with the lean veal: clean it of tendons and skin if present, and mince it very finely with a small piece of prosciutto fat from the 40 grams. To this minced meat add the garlic and parsley, minced very finely, the Parmesan, the egg, a pinch of pepper, very little salt and mix everything well together. If you have the opportunity, mince a truffle ball into the mixture.
2. Debone the breast of hard bones and leave the tender ones; open it in the connective tissue by passing the knife under the ribs so that it becomes double in surface area like opening a book. Over the half of the breast where the tender bones remain, spread part of the mixture and over this arrange part of the prosciutto or mortadella cut into strips one finger wide, spacing them at small distances from one another. Over this first layer place a second and third, if you have enough filling, always alternating the mixture and cured meats. Once finished, pull the other half of the breast that remained bare over the filling to close it, as it were, like a book, and with a large needle and thread sew the edges so the filling does not burst out; besides this, tie it tightly in a cross with string.
3. Put it on the fire in a pot with a piece of butter, salt and pepper, and when it has taken color on both sides, bring it to cooking temperature with water poured in little by little.
4. Serve it hot with its reduced sauce; but first remove the string and thread. If it has turned out well it should be able to be cut into slices and make a fine appearance with its lardons. You can surround it with fresh peas cooked in their own sauce, or with fennel cut into sections, but first boiled.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pezzo in Gelo (Frozen Biscuit)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Water, 140 grams
- Sugar, 50 grams
- Egg yolks, 4
- Vanilla flavoring
- Fish glue (isinglass), 2 sheets
- Whipped cream, 150 grams
- Ice and salt for freezing

## Directions

1. Make a cream with water, sugar, egg yolks, and vanilla flavoring
2. Put it on the fire, stirring continuously, and when it begins to stick to the wooden spoon, remove it and whip it with a whisk
3. If it takes too long to whip, keep the basin on ice
4. Gradually add two sheets of fish glue dissolved on the fire in a little water
5. Once whipped, slowly add 150 grams of whipped cream to the mixture
6. Place the mixture in a mold made for frozen pieces or in a copper pot or vessel, completely covered
7. Let it freeze for at least three hours, surrounded by a thick layer of ice and salt
8. This quantity will serve seven or eight people and makes a very pleasing dessert
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piccione a Sorpresa (Surprise Pigeon)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One pigeon
- One veal cutlet or milk veal cutlet
- Salt
- Pepper
- A pinch of spices
- Butter
- Truffles (optional)
- The pigeon's liver and heart
- One chicken (as alternative to pigeon)

## Directions

1. If you have a pigeon to put on the spit and want it to serve more than one person, fill it with a veal cutlet or milk veal cutlet of proportionate size.
2. Beat the cutlet well to make it thinner and more tender, season it with salt, pepper, a pinch of spices and some pieces of butter, roll it up and place it inside the pigeon, sewing the opening closed.
3. If you add slices of truffles to the seasoning mentioned above, it will be even better.
4. You can also cook separately the small onion and the pigeon's liver in sauce or butter, mash them and use them to spread on the cutlet; in this way the different aroma of the two types of meat blends together and forms a better flavor.
5. What has been said for the pigeon applies equally to a chicken.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piccione all'inglese (English-style Squab)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One young but large squab
- Lean veal, 100 grams, or one chicken breast
- Thin slices of fatty and lean ham, 40 grams
- Slices of salted tongue, 30 grams
- Butter, 40 grams
- Half a glass of good defatted broth
- One hard-boiled egg
- Flour, 150 grams
- Butter, 50 grams
- Spirit of wine, one teaspoon
- Sugar, one teaspoon
- Lemon juice, one wedge
- One egg yolk
- Salt, as needed
- Pepper, as needed
- Spices, a hint

## Directions

1. Cut the squab into small pieces at its joints, discarding the head and feet.
2. Cut the veal or chicken breast into small rolled pieces and beat them with the back of a knife.
3. Cut the ham and tongue into strips one finger wide.
4. Cut the egg into eight wedges.
5. Take an oval metal or porcelain plate that can withstand fire and arrange in layers one on top of the other: first half of the squab and veal, then half of the ham and tongue, half of the butter scattered here and there in small pieces, and half, that is four wedges, of the egg; season with very little salt, pepper, and a hint of spices, and repeat the operation with the remainder so that the whole makes a mound.
6. Finally pour the aforementioned broth, but cold, which you will see floating on the first edge of the plate and which will remain largely after cooking.
7. For the pastry: mix the flour with the aforementioned ingredients (spirit of wine, sugar, lemon juice, egg yolk, and salt), and if they are not enough, add tepid water to make a rather soft dough.
8. Work it very much by throwing it with force against the pastry board, let it rest a little, and roll it out by folding it four or five times, reducing it finally to the thickness of a shield with the striped rolling pin.
9. Cover the plate with it, adorning it, if possible, with trimmings of the same pastry, then gild it with egg yolk.
10. Cook this pastry (which can be called such) in a country oven and serve it hot.
11. Alternatively, give the squab and other meats a partial cooking first with the butter, seasoning them with salt, pepper, and spices, then arrange them on the platter in the indicated manner, not excluding the cooking juices and broth. You can increase the seasoning and also add chicken giblets, sweetbreads, and truffles.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piccione coi Piselli (Squab with Peas)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Squabs
- Peas
- Onion
- Ham
- Oil
- Butter
- Water or broth

## Directions

1. They say the best way to cook squabs is stewed with peas. Cook them in a stew with a finely chopped mixture of onion, ham, oil and butter, placing the squabs on top, wetting them with water or broth when they have taken color on all sides to finish cooking them. Pass the gravy, degrease it and in the same cook the peas with which you will garnish the squabs when serving them to table.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piccione in Gratella (Pigeon on the Grill)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one large young pigeon
- oil
- salt
- pepper
- 40 grams of butter
- one egg
- breadcrumbs

## Directions

1. Take a large but young pigeon, divide it in two lengthwise and flatten the pieces well with your hands.
2. Then place them to fry in oil for four or five minutes, just enough to firm up the meat.
3. Season it while hot with salt and pepper, then prepare it in this manner: melt 40 grams of butter over heat without letting it boil; beat an egg and mix one with the other.
4. Dip the pigeon well in this mixture and after some time roll it entirely in breadcrumbs.
5. Cook it on the grill over slow heat and serve it with a sauce or side dish.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piccioni in Umido (Braised Pigeons)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- pigeons
- whole sage leaves
- slices of fat and lean prosciutto
- oil
- salt
- pepper
- butter
- broth
- lemon
- toasted bread slices
- verjuice (optional alternative to lemon)

## Directions

1. Garnish the pigeons with whole sage leaves, place them in a pan or pot over slices of fat and lean prosciutto and season them with oil, salt and pepper.
2. When they have taken color, add a piece of butter and bring them to cook with broth.
3. Before removing them from the fire squeeze a lemon over them and use their juice to serve them with slices of toasted bread placed underneath.
4. Note to salt them very little because of the prosciutto and the broth.
5. At the time of verjuice, you can use the latter instead of lemon.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piselli alla Francese I (Peas French Style I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fresh peas, 1 liter
- Young onions, 2
- Parsley stems
- Butter, 30 grams at first, then another 30 grams
- Broth, as needed
- Lettuce, 2 whole heads
- Salt
- Pepper
- Flour, 1 tablespoon not heaping
- Egg yolks, 2

## Directions

1. Take two young onions, cut them in half lengthwise, enclose some parsley stems in the middle and tie them.
2. Put them on the fire with 30 grams of butter and when browned, pour over them a good ladleful of broth.
3. Let boil and when the onions are very soft, strain them, squeezing them together with the juice, which you will return to the fire with the peas and two whole heads of lettuce.
4. Season with salt and pepper and let them boil slowly.
5. At the halfway point of cooking add another 30 grams of butter mixed with one tablespoon not heaping of flour and pour broth if needed.
6. Before serving, bind them with two egg yolks dissolved in a little broth. In this way they turn out very delicate.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piselli alla Francese II (Peas French Style II)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Onion
- Butter
- Flour, a pinch
- Broth, 1 or 2 ladlefuls
- Peas
- Salt
- Pepper
- Lettuce, 1 or 2 whole heads
- Sugar, 1 teaspoon (optional)

## Directions

1. Slice a good amount of onion into very thin slices and put it on the fire in a pot with a piece of butter.
2. When well browned, pour in a pinch of flour, stir, and then add, depending on the quantity, one or two ladlefuls of broth and let the flour cook.
3. Pour in the peas, season with salt and pepper, and at the halfway point of cooking add one or two whole heads of lettuce.
4. Boil slowly, taking care that the liquid does not become too thick.
5. Some sweeten the peas with a teaspoon of sugar; but in this case use little, because the sweetness should seem natural and not added artificially.
6. When you serve them remove the lettuce.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piselli col Prosciutto (Peas with Prosciutto)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Peas
- Young onions, 1 or 2
- Oil
- Prosciutto, fatty and lean, cut into small dice
- Salt, little or none
- Pepper
- Broth
- Butter, a little

## Directions

1. Divide one or two young onions in half lengthwise, according to the quantity of peas, and put them on the fire with oil and some prosciutto, both fatty and lean, cut into small dice.
2. Sauté until the prosciutto is shriveled; then throw in the peas, season with little or no salt and a pinch of pepper; stir and finish cooking them with broth, adding a little butter.
3. Serve them, either alone as a legume dish or as a side dish; but first discard all the onion.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Piselli colla Carnesecca (Peas with Dried Meat)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Dried meat (carnesecca)
- Garlic
- Parsley
- Oil
- Salt
- Pepper
- Broth or water

## Directions

1. Put on the fire a fine mixture of dried meat, garlic, parsley and oil; season with little salt and pepper, and when the garlic has taken on color, throw in the peas.
2. Once they have absorbed the fat, finish cooking them with broth or, in its absence, with water.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pizza a Libretti (Folded Fried Pizza)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- two eggs
- salt, a pinch
- cognac or spirits, three tablespoons (or fumetto)
- butter, 20 grams
- oil or lard for frying
- powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Roll out a sheet of dough not too firm and as thin as possible by mixing flour with two eggs, a pinch of salt and three tablespoons of cognac or spirits, or perhaps better fumetto.
2. Once the sheet is made, grease it with 20 grams of melted butter and roll it, or fold it over itself to the width of 10 to 11 centimeters, but so that the greased part remains inside; then cut the roll in half lengthwise and then crosswise at proportionate distances to obtain many rectangles and press with your fingers the outer edge of these, that is the rib that has not been cut.
3. Fry them in a pan with plenty of fat and before serving dust them with powdered sugar.
4. If they turn out well you will see that these little books open and remain flaked.
5. This dose can be enough for four people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pizza alla Napoletana (Neapolitan Pizza)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Short crust pastry (half the recipe A of number 589, or the entire recipe B of the same number)
- Ricotta cheese, 150 grams
- Sweet almonds with three bitter ones, 70 grams
- Sugar, 50 grams
- Flour, 20 grams
- Eggs, 1 whole and 1 yolk
- Lemon zest or vanilla flavoring
- Milk, half a glass

## Directions

1. Make a cream with the milk, sugar, flour, and the whole egg mentioned, and when it is cooked and still boiling add the yolk and give it flavor
2. Add to the cream the ricotta and the blanched and finely pounded almonds
3. Mix everything and fill with this mixture the short crust pastry arranged like a tart, that is between two sheets of the same pastry decorated on top and brushed with egg yolk
4. It should be baked in the oven, served cold and dusted with powdered sugar
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pizza Gravida (Pregnant Pizza)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Milk, one quarter liter
- Sugar, 60 grams
- Cornstarch, 30 grams
- Egg yolks, 2 numbers
- Flavoring of your preference
- Whole pine nuts, 30 grams
- Raisins, 80 grams
- Short crust pastry

## Directions

1. Prepare a cream using the milk, sugar, cornstarch, egg yolks, and flavoring
2. When you remove it from the fire, add the whole pine nuts and raisins
3. Fill a short crust pastry with this mixture as you did for pizza alla napoletana
4. Cook it as you did the previous one
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pizza Gravida (Pregnant Pizza)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Milk, one quarter liter
- Sugar, 60 grams
- Starch, 30 grams
- Egg yolks, 2
- Flavoring of your choice

## Directions

1. Prepare this mixture as a cream
2. Add it when you remove it from the fire
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Plum-cake (English Plum Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Sugar, 250 grams
- Butter, 250 grams
- Finest flour, 250 grams
- Candied fruit, 80 grams
- Malaga raisins, 80 grams
- Sultana raisins, 80 grams
- Passolina raisins, 80 grams
- Whole eggs, 5
- Egg yolks, 4
- Rum, slightly less than one deciliter, that is five tablespoonfuls
- Flavor of lemon zest or vanilla

## Directions

1. Cut the candied fruit into thin strips and remove the seeds from the malaga raisins
2. First work the butter alone with a spoon, softening it at the heat if necessary, add the sugar and continue working it until it has become white
3. Break the eggs one at a time mixing them in, then the flour and finally the remaining ingredients
4. Pour the mixture into a smooth mold that has been previously lined with paper buttered on the inside and bake it in the oven
5. You can serve it hot sprinkled with powdered sugar, and also cold, which is equally good
6. The paper serves to prevent the raisins from sticking to the mold. This dose will be enough for twelve people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Plum-pudding (English Plum Pudding)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- For every egg used to bind it, the following ingredients in these quantities:
- Powdered sugar, 30 grams
- Zibibbo raisins, 30 grams
- Sultana raisins, 30 grams
- Crumb of fine bread, 30 grams
- Suet from wethered mutton, 30 grams
- Candied citron, 15 grams
- Candied orange, 15 grams
- Rum, one tablespoonfull

## Directions

1. Remove seeds from the zibibbo raisins; cut the candied fruits into short, thin strips; if you cannot obtain the suet from a castrated ram, take it from veal, and cut both this and the bread crumb into very small dice, removing the membranes from the suet
2. Make a mixture of everything, having previously beaten the eggs separately, and let it rest for some hours; then place it in a cloth and tie it very tightly with string to form a ball
3. Place a pot of water on the heat and when it boils immerse the pudding in it in such a way that it does not touch the bottom of the pot, letting it boil slowly for as many hours as there are eggs
4. Remove it from the cloth carefully, make a small well on top and, pouring in it a small glass or two of cognac or rum that spreads throughout the pudding, set it alight and thus hot and in flames send it to the table to be cut into slices and eaten when the flame is extinguished
5. Three eggs will be enough for six people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polenta di Farina Gialla colle Salsicce (Cornmeal Polenta with Sausages)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Corn flour
- Sausages
- Water
- Tomato sauce or tomato preserve
- Parmesan cheese
- Butter

## Directions

1. Make a rather soft polenta from cornmeal, spread it on a work surface to the thickness of a finger, and cut it into almond shapes.
2. Place several whole sausages in a small pan with a drop of water and when cooked, peel them, crumble them, and add tomato sauce or tomato preserve.
3. Place the polenta in a baking dish or tray that can withstand fire.
4. Season it layer by layer with Parmesan, the sausages, and some small pieces of butter scattered here and there.
5. Then place it between two fires and when well heated, serve it, especially as a first course for a fork breakfast. The said polenta can also be made firm to cut into slices.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polenta Pasticciata (Baked Layered Cornmeal Polenta)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Corn flour
- Milk
- Salt
- Parmesan cheese
- Butter
- Bolognese sauce or similar
- Béchamel sauce

## Directions

1. Make a firm polenta from corn flour cooked in milk.
2. Salt it when you are about to remove it from the fire and pour it onto a work surface, about two fingers high.
3. When cooled, cut it into large almond-shaped pieces about half a centimeter thick, which you will arrange in the following manner in a metal or porcelain tray that can withstand fire.
4. Make a sauce like the one for seasoning Bolognese-style macaroni or similar, and make some béchamel sauce.
5. Dust the bottom of the tray with grated Parmesan and spread a layer of polenta; season it with Parmesan, the sauce, and the béchamel; then on top of this place another layer of polenta and season it equally; and so on until you run out of ingredients.
6. A few small pieces of butter here and there will not hurt, but use sparingly if you do not want it to become cloying from excessive seasoning.
7. Prepared this way with its top, place it in a country oven to brown the polenta and serve it hot as a side dish during a meal in autumn and winter.
8. If it turns out well it will be praised for its delicacy.
9. During hunting season a skilled cook can put it in form, filling it with small birds cooked in their own juices.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollastra in Umido col Contorno di Riso (Stewed Hen with Rice Border)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One hen, empty, weighing approximately 700 grams
- Rice, 300 grams
- Butter, 100 grams
- Fat and lean ham, 40 grams
- One onion, more than medium size
- One piece of carrot
- One handful of dried mushrooms

## Directions

1. Tie the hen to hold its parts together.
2. Put 30 grams of the butter and the ham cut into strips in a pot.
3. Chop the onion and carrot over it.
4. Place the hen breast-side down in the pot, seasoning it with salt and pepper.
5. Keep it covered and when browned on both sides, moisten it gradually with hot water until fully cooked, leaving enough sauce to flavor the rice, but strain the sauce.
6. Put the rice on the fire as is with half of the remaining butter.
7. Draw it to completion with hot water and finally with the hen's sauce.
8. When fully cooked, add the rest of the butter and give it greater flavor with a good handful of grated Parmesan.
9. Cook the liver and gizzard together with the hen and cut into pieces, mix them into the rice.
10. The risotto thus prepared can also serve as a soup and be enough for three people, but then serve the hen separately with some of its sauce and the mushrooms as a side dish.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo al Diavolo (Deviled Chicken)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One young rooster or young chicken
- Butter or oil
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Take a young rooster or young chicken, remove the neck and legs, and open it completely along the front, flattening it as much as possible.
2. Wash and dry it well with a cloth, then place it on a grill.
3. When it begins to brown, turn it, brush it with melted butter or oil using a brush, and season it with salt and pepper.
4. When the opposite side begins to take color, turn it and treat it in the same manner.
5. Continue brushing it with butter or oil and seasoning it sufficiently, keeping it over the fire until it is cooked.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo alla Cacciatora (Hunter-Style Chicken)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one large onion
- oil or lard
- one chicken
- salt
- pepper
- half a glass of Sangiovese or other good red wine
- some tomato juice

## Directions

1. Slice a large onion and soak it in fresh water for more than half an hour, then dry it and put it in a pan with oil or lard.
2. When cooked, set it aside.
3. Cut up a chicken and fry it in the remaining fat, and when browned, add the said onion.
4. Season with salt and pepper and pour in half a glass of Sangiovese or other good red wine and some tomato juice.
5. After five minutes of boiling, serve it.
6. Note that this is not a dish for weak stomachs.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo alla Contadina (Chicken Country-Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One young hen
- Sprigs of rosemary
- One garlic clove, divided into four or five pieces
- Lardoon, minced
- Salt
- Pepper
- Tomatoes, in pieces
- Potatoes, raw
- Oil or lard or butter

## Directions

1. Lard the chicken with some sprigs of rosemary and with the garlic clove divided into four or five pieces.
2. Put it on the fire with minced lardoon and season it with salt and pepper inside and outside.
3. When it is browned on all sides, add tomatoes in pieces, having removed the seeds.
4. When these are broken down, moisten it with broth or water.
5. Separately in oil, lard or butter, brown some raw potatoes cut into wedges.
6. Let them take flavor in the chicken's sauce and serve them as a side dish.
7. Substitute butter for the lardoon if you want the chicken with a more delicate taste.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo alla Marengo (Chicken Marengo)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one young chicken, excluding the neck and legs
- 30 grams of butter
- one spoonful of oil
- salt
- pepper
- a pinch of nutmeg
- one level spoonful of flour
- one decilitre of white wine
- broth
- parsley, finely chopped
- half a lemon

## Directions

1. Take a young chicken and, excluding the neck and legs, cut it into large pieces at the joints.
2. Sauté it with 30 grams of butter, one spoonful of oil and season it with salt, pepper and a pinch of nutmeg.
3. Once the pieces are browned on both sides, drain off the fat and throw into the sauté pan one level spoonful of flour and one decilitre of white wine.
4. Add broth to cook the chicken through, covered, and over low heat.
5. Before removing it from the fire, garnish it with a pinch of chopped parsley and when it is on the platter squeeze half a lemon over it. It turns out to be an appetizing dish.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo alla Rudinì (Chicken Rudinì)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One young chicken
- One egg
- Water
- Flour
- Pepper
- Salt
- 100 grams of butter
- Lemon

## Directions

1. Take a young chicken, remove the neck, the wing tips, and cut the legs two fingers from the knee; then cut it into six pieces: two with the wings to each of which you will leave attached half of the breast, two with the thighs including the hip, and two with the back with the front part removed.
2. Remove the hip bones and the breast wishbone; crush the two back pieces.
3. Beat an egg with water equal to what fits in half an eggshell, place the chicken (after flouring it) in infusion in this mixture, and season it with pepper and salt in good measure, leaving it until the moment of cooking.
4. Then take the pieces one by one, coat them in breadcrumbs, and with a sauté pan or copper skillet over the fire with 100 grams of butter, cook them in this manner.
5. When the butter begins to sizzle, place the chicken pieces skin side down for a moment, then turn them over, cover the pan with a lid and with much heat above and little below, leave them for about ten minutes.
6. Serve it with lemon wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Colla Marsala (Chicken with Marsala)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One chicken, cut into large pieces
- Onion, finely chopped, minced
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper
- Broth
- Marsala

## Directions

1. Cut the chicken into large pieces and put it in a pot with minced onion and a piece of butter.
2. Season it with salt and pepper.
3. When it is well browned, add some broth and cook it through.
4. Strain the sauce, degrease it if necessary and put the chicken back on the fire with some Marsala.
5. Remove it as soon as it returns to a boil.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo colle Salsicce (Chicken with Sausages)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- half an onion, finely chopped
- a piece of butter
- four or five slices of prosciutto, one finger wide
- one whole chicken
- pepper
- salt
- broth or water
- three or four fresh whole sausages

## Directions

1. Finely chop half an onion and place it in a casserole with a piece of butter and four or five slices of prosciutto, one finger wide.
2. Place a whole chicken on top of these ingredients, season it with pepper and a little salt, and put it on the fire.
3. Let it take on color from all sides, and when the onion is completely rendered, moisten it with broth or water and add three or four fresh whole sausages.
4. Let it cook over low heat, making sure that at the end there remains some liquid.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo con la Panna (Chicken with Cream)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one piece of young chicken breast
- oil
- salt
- one quarter of a medium-sized onion
- 50 grams of butter
- 10 grams of flour
- three decilitres of cream, or if this is lacking, the same amount of very good milk

## Directions

1. Put a piece of young chicken breast on the spit to give it two-thirds of roasting time; oil it, salt it and let it take on color.
2. Then divide it at its joints and cut the breast into two pieces to finish cooking it in the following manner.
3. Chop a quarter of a medium-sized onion and put it on the fire with 50 grams of butter; when it is well browned, throw in 10 grams of flour and then, little by little, three decilitres of cream or, if this is lacking, the same amount of very good milk.
4. When you believe the flour is cooked, pour in the pieces of chicken to finish cooking them.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Disossato Ripieno (Boned and Stuffed Chicken)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One chicken
- Veal loin, approximately 300 grams (or proportionally less if the chicken is small)
- Bread crumb, one large piece soaked in broth
- Parmesan cheese, grated, one handful
- Egg yolks, 3
- Salt
- Pepper
- Nutmeg (optional)
- Ham, fat and lean, 20 grams
- Salted tongue, 20 grams
- Butter
- Onion
- Celery
- Carrot
- Water

## Directions

1. Cut the neck in half, the wing tips, and the legs at the thigh joint.
2. Open the chicken along the back superficially, from the wings to the tail, and with a sharp knife begin to remove the bones of the wings from the inside, scraping the meat well.
3. Remove the bones of the hips and thighs from the inside.
4. Scraping with the knife the external bones of the carcass, remove it all in one piece, including the innards.
5. Leave the small bones of the breastbone, or remove it all and take out the breast fork.
6. Turn the thighs and wings, now stripped of bones, drawing them inward and remove all the tendons you find between the meat.
7. Finely mince the veal, then pound it in a mortar to reduce it very fine.
8. Add to this meat a large piece of bread soaked in broth, a handful of grated Parmesan, three egg yolks, salt, pepper and, if you like, nutmeg flavor.
9. Add to the mixture 20 grams of fat and lean ham and 20 grams of salted tongue, cut into small dice.
10. Fill the chicken and sew it closed.
11. Wrap it tightly in cloth and tie it.
12. Put it to cook in water for a couple of hours over low heat.
13. Remove the wrapping and let it brown first with butter, then in a sauce made as follows:
14. Break all the bones removed from the chicken, the neck and head included, and with dried meat in pieces, butter, onion, celery and carrot put them on the fire in a pot.
15. Season with salt and pepper, draw the sauce with the water in which the chicken has boiled, which has already become a good broth.
16. Before serving, alone or with a side dish, remove the thread with which it was sewn.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Dorato I (Gilded Chicken I)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Young chicken
- Flour
- Eggs, 2
- Breadcrumbs
- Oil or pure lard
- Salt and pepper
- Lemon wedges

## Directions

1. Take a young chicken, clean it, remove the head and feet, wash it well and keep it in boiling water for one minute.
2. Cut it into pieces at its joints, coat it with flour, season with salt and pepper and pour two beaten eggs over it.
3. After at least half an hour of soaking wrap the pieces in breadcrumbs, repeating the operation twice if necessary and cook them on hot coals in this manner: take a sauté pan or a tinned copper pan, put oil or better pure lard in it, and when it begins to sizzle throw down the pieces of chicken making them brown on both sides over moderate heat so the cooking penetrates inside.
4. Serve them hot with lemon wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Dorato II (Gilded Chicken II)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Young chicken
- Flour
- Eggs, 2
- Salt and pepper
- Lemon wedges

## Directions

1. After treating it as the previous recipe, cut it into smaller pieces, coat with flour, immerse it in two beaten and well-salted eggs; fry it in a pan, season it still a little with salt and pepper, and serve it with lemon wedges.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Fritto coi Pomodori (Fried Chicken with Tomatoes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one chicken
- sufficient quantity of lard
- salt
- pepper
- tomatoes

## Directions

1. Cut the chicken into small pieces and put it in a pan as is with sufficient quantity of lard, seasoning with salt and pepper.
2. When cooked, drain off the excess fat and add the tomatoes in small pieces after removing the seeds.
3. Stir continuously until the tomatoes have almost dissolved and serve.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo in Porchetta (Chicken Cooked Like Pork)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One chicken
- Slices of fat and lean prosciutto, slightly more than a finger's width wide
- Three whole garlic cloves
- Two small sprigs of fennel
- Several peppercorns
- Salt
- Pepper
- Butter

## Directions

1. Fill the chicken with slices of fat and lean prosciutto, add three whole garlic cloves, two small sprigs of fennel, and several peppercorns.
2. Season the outside with salt and pepper.
3. Cook it in a pot with only butter, using heat both above and below, until cooked.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo in Salsa d'Uovo (Chicken in Egg Sauce)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one young chicken, cut into pieces
- 50 grams of butter
- salt
- pepper
- a pinch of flour
- broth
- one egg yolk
- juice of half a lemon

## Directions

1. Cut a young chicken into pieces and place it in a casserole with 50 grams of butter.
2. Season it with salt and pepper.
3. When it has sautéed somewhat, sprinkle a pinch of flour over it to help it take on color, then cook it through with broth.
4. Remove it to a platter, keeping it warm, and into the remaining sauce pour an egg yolk, beaten beforehand with the juice of half a lemon, to form the sauce.
5. Stir it somewhat over the fire, pour it over the chicken and serve.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo in Salsa Tonnata (Chicken in Tuna Sauce)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- one young chicken breast (cleaned of organs, neck, and legs), approximately 600 grams raw
- Tuna in oil, 50 grams
- Capers squeezed of vinegar, 30 grams
- Anchovies, 3
- Parsley, one handful (enough to give green color to the sauce)
- four tablespoons of oil
- half tablespoon of vinegar
- salt
- pepper
- two hard-boiled eggs for garnish

## Directions

1. Boil the chicken breast in water for half an hour until cooked through.
2. Remove the skin from the cooked chicken, debone it completely, and cut it into pieces.
3. Season the chicken pieces with salt (sparingly), pepper, and two tablespoons of oil.
4. Let the chicken sit on a platter for several hours.
5. Clean the anchovies of scales and bones. Finely chop the parsley with a mincing knife.
6. Pound the anchovies, parsley, tuna, and capers together in a mortar until the mixture is very fine.
7. Transfer the sauce to a bowl and dilute it with four tablespoons of oil and half a tablespoon of vinegar.
8. Coat half of the sauce over the chicken pieces and cover with the remaining half.
9. Garnish with hard-boiled eggs cut into wedges around the chicken when serving.
10. Serves six people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Ripieno Arrosto (Roasted Stuffed Chicken)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One medium-sized chicken
- Two sausages
- The chicken's liver, crest and wattles
- Eight or ten well-roasted chestnuts
- One ball of truffles or, in their absence, some pieces of dried mushrooms
- The aroma of nutmeg
- One egg
- Bread crumb
- Broth
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Start by giving the sausages and giblets half-cooking in butter, moistening them with a little broth if needed; season with a little salt and a little pepper because of the sausages.
2. Remove them dry and in the remaining liquid throw a bread crumb to obtain, with a little broth, two spoonfuls of thick paste.
3. Skin the sausages, mince with the mezzaluna the giblets and the softened mushrooms, and together with the burnt pieces, the egg and the paste, pound everything very finely in a mortar, except the truffles which should be cut into slices and left raw.
4. This is the mixture with which you will fill the chicken, whose filling will be cut better when cold than hot and will also be more pleasing to the taste.
5. If it were a turkey instead of a chicken, double the dose.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pollo Vestito (Dressed Chicken)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- The breast of a young chicken, without legs, neck, and innards
- Butter
- Salt
- Pasta (from recipe No. 277)
- Egg yolk

## Directions

1. Take the breast of a young chicken, without legs, neck, and innards, grease it all with cold butter, dust it with salt, and add a pinch of salt inside.
2. With the wings folded, wrap it with two wide and thin slices of lean prosciutto and cover it with pastry described in recipe No. 277, rolled out with a rolling pin to approximately the thickness of a coin.
3. Brush it with egg yolk and cook the dressed chicken at moderate heat in an oven or camp oven.
4. Serve it as it is to be opened and carved at table.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polpette (Meatballs)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Cooked meat, left over
- Prosciutto, a slice fat and lean
- Parmesan cheese
- Salt
- Pepper
- Spices
- Raisins
- Pine nuts
- Breadcrumb paste, made with marrow cooked in broth or milk
- Egg
- Breadcrumbs
- Oil or lard
- Garlic
- Parsley
- Egg yolk and lemon juice sauce

## Directions

1. Chop the cooked meat with a mezzaluna and chop separately a slice of fat and lean prosciutto to mix with it.
2. Season with parmesan, salt, pepper, spices, raisins, pine nuts, and some spoonfuls of paste made with bread marrow cooked in broth or milk, binding the mixture with one or two eggs depending on quantity.
3. Form many balls the size of an egg, flattened at the poles like the globe.
4. Coat them with breadcrumbs and fry them in oil or lard.
5. Then with a small soffritto of garlic and parsley and the fat left in the pan, pass them to a pan, adorning them with an egg and lemon juice sauce.
6. Alternatively, place them in the pan with a piece of butter.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polpette di Trippa (Tripe Meatballs)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Boiled tripe, 350 grams
- Ham, leaner rather than fatty, 100 grams
- Grated parmesan cheese, 30 grams
- Beef marrow, 20 grams
- Eggs, 2
- A good pinch of parsley
- Spice or nutmeg flavoring
- Non-liquid breadcrumb mixture made of bread soaked in broth or milk, two spoonfuls
- Oil or lard for frying
- Onion, just under one quarter of a medium-sized onion
- Butter, 60 grams
- Tomato juice or tomato conserve dissolved in broth
- Parmesan cheese

## Directions

1. Mince the tripe as finely as possible with the chopping tool
2. Do the same with the ham, marrow and parsley, add the eggs, the rest, a little salt and mix
3. Form 12 or 13 meatballs from this mixture, which will be enough for four people, coat well with flour and fry them in oil or lard
4. Make a small soffritto with the onion and place it in a proportioned pan with 60 grams of butter
5. When colored, place the meatballs in it, moisten them after a short time with tomato juice or conserve dissolved in broth, cover and let them boil gently for about ten minutes, turning them
6. Then serve them with some of their sauce and dust with parmesan
7. The author adds raisins and pine nuts to the mixture, but this is optional
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polpettone (Meatloaf)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Cooked meat, left over
- Salt
- Pepper
- Parmesan cheese
- Egg
- Breadcrumb paste, made with bread marrow cooked in milk, broth, or water with a little butter
- Flour
- Lard

## Directions

1. Remove the fat and chop the lean meat with a mezzaluna.
2. Season and proportion it with salt, pepper, parmesan, one or two eggs, and two or three spoonfuls of paste.
3. The paste may be made from bread marrow cooked in milk, broth, or simply in water graced with a little butter.
4. Mix everything together, form it into an oval loaf and flour it.
5. Then fry it in lard.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polpettone di Carne Cruda alla Fiorentina (Raw Meat Meatloaf Florentine Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- half a kilogram of lean veal without bone
- one slice of fat and lean prosciutto
- salt
- pepper
- spices
- one egg
- flour
- onion (about the size of a walnut)
- parsley
- celery
- carrot
- a small piece of butter
- half a generous glass of water
- half a teaspoon of flour
- half a lemon

## Directions

1. Take half a kilogram of lean veal without bone, clean it of membranes and calluses, and mince it finely first with a knife, then with a crescent-shaped knife, together with a slice of fat and lean prosciutto.
2. Season with a little salt, pepper, and spices, add one egg, mix well, and with wet hands form it into a ball and dust with flour.
3. Make a small battuto with a little onion (about the size of a walnut), parsley, celery, and carrot, put it on the fire with a small piece of butter, and when it has taken color, throw in the meatloaf.
4. Brown it on all sides, then pour into the pan half a generous glass of water in which you have dissolved half a teaspoon of flour; cover it and let it boil over the lowest heat, watching that it does not stick.
5. When serving, squeeze half a lemon over it with its dense sauce around it.
6. If you want it Piedmont style, simply place a peeled hard-boiled egg in the center of the ball when forming it, which serves to give beauty to the meatloaf when sliced.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Polpettone di Lesso (Meatloaf from Leftover Boiled Meat)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- leftover boiled meat
- salt
- pepper
- parmesan cheese
- one or two eggs
- two or three tablespoons of bread paste
- bread marrow cooked in milk, broth, or water with a little butter
- lard or oil for frying
- butter
- two eggs
- salt
- half a lemon

## Directions

1. Remove the fat from the leftover meat and mince the lean part with a crescent-shaped knife.
2. Season it proportionally with salt, pepper, parmesan cheese, one or two eggs, and two or three tablespoons of bread paste made from bread marrow cooked in milk, broth, or water enriched with a little butter.
3. Mix everything together, form it into an oval loaf, and dust it with flour.
4. Fry it in lard or oil until it becomes firm and forms a crust on the surface.
5. Remove from the pan and sauté it in butter on both sides in a pan.
6. When ready to serve, bind it with two beaten eggs, a pinch of salt, and half a lemon.
7. Make this sauce separately in a small pot, treating it like a cream, and pour it over the meatloaf when it is placed on a serving dish.
8. If the meatloaf is large, turn it in the pan using a plate or copper lid as you would for an omelet.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pomodori Ripieni (Stuffed Tomatoes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Medium-sized ripe tomatoes
- Onion
- Parsley
- Celery
- Butter
- Dried mushrooms
- Milk
- Salt
- Pepper
- Parmesan
- Egg
- Garlic
- Breadcrumbs
- Oil

## Directions

1. Take medium-sized ripe tomatoes and cut them in half equally, remove the seeds, season with salt and pepper and fill the holes formed with the following mixture, so that it overflows and rises heaped on the surface of the tomato.
2. Make a battutino with onion, parsley and celery, put it on the fire with a piece of butter and when it takes color, pour in a handful of softened and finely chopped dried mushrooms; add a spoonful of pap with milk, season with salt and pepper, and boil somewhat, moistening the mixture with milk if needed.
3. Remove from fire and when lukewarm add grated Parmesan and an egg or just the yolk if sufficient to make the mixture not too liquid.
4. Cook the prepared tomatoes in a pan over two fires with a little butter and oil together and serve them as a side dish to any roast or grilled steak.
5. For serving alongside boiled meat they are excellent prepared as follows: take a wide pan or a baking sheet, scatter small pieces of butter on it and on top place, skin-side up, tomatoes cut in half after removing the seeds. Season with salt, pepper and a little oil, scatter more small pieces of butter on top and cook them in an uncovered pan.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ponce alla Parigina (Parisian Punch)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- One cup of about two deciliters
- One egg yolk
- Two teaspoons of sugar
- Two or three tablespoons of cognac, rum, or other liqueur to taste
- Boiling water

## Directions

1. Take a cup of about two deciliters capacity
2. Whisk in an egg yolk with two teaspoons of sugar, beating until it becomes almost white
3. Add, seasoning to taste, two or three tablespoons of cognac, rum, or other liqueur you prefer
4. Fill the cup with boiling water poured gradually while continuing to whisk to create foam
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ponce alla Romana (Roman Punch Ice Cream)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Zucchero (sugar), 450 grams
- Acqua (water), 5 deciliters
- Aranci (oranges), 2 number
- Limoni (lemons), 2 number
- Chiare d'uovo (egg whites), 2 number
- Rhum (rum), one small glass
- Odore di vaniglia (vanilla flavoring)

## Directions

1. Boil for five or six minutes 250 grams of the said sugar in 4 deciliters of the said water, with a little peel of lemon and orange inside
2. Remove from fire, squeeze the juice of the oranges and lemons into this syrup, strain it through a cloth and pour it into the ice cream maker to freeze
3. Put on the fire the remaining 200 grams of sugar in the remaining deciliter of water, give it the vanilla flavoring, and let it boil until the point that pouring a drop onto a plate it remains round, and taken between the fingers makes threads; but first you will have beaten the two egg whites until very stiff, onto which you will pour the sugar thus boiling, beating them to form a united paste which you will throw, when cooled, into the ice cream already ready, mixing well; at the moment of serving add the rum and serve it at table in small glasses
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ponce di arancio (Orange Punch)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Rum, 1-1/2 liters
- Alcohol, 1 liter
- Water, 1 liter
- Fine white sugar, 1 kilogram
- Juice of three oranges
- The grated rind of one garden lemon kept in infusion for three days in one deciliter of the said alcohol

## Directions

1. Put the water and sugar on the fire and let it boil for five or six minutes
2. When it is cold add the rum, the juice of the oranges and the alcohol, including that of the infusion passed through cloth
3. Filter it like the other liqueurs and bottle it
4. It is customary to serve it lit in small glasses
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Presnitz (Triestine Filled Pastry)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 160 grams sultana raisins
- 130 grams sugar
- 130 grams shelled walnuts
- 110 grams stale focaccia
- 60 grams shelled sweet almonds
- 60 grams pine nuts
- 35 grams candied citron
- 35 grams candied orange
- 5 grams mixed spices of cinnamon, cloves and mace
- 2 grams salt
- 1 deciliter Cyprus wine
- 1 deciliter rum
- 160 grams flour
- 80 grams butter

## Directions

1. After cleaning the sultana raisins, soak them in the Cyprus wine and rum mixed together; let them sit like this for several hours and remove them when they begin to swell.
2. Cut the pine nuts into three parts crosswise, cut the candied fruits into very small dice, chop the walnuts and almonds with a crescent blade to about the size of rice, and the focaccia, which can be a dough similar to brioches or Milanese panettone, grate or crumble it.
3. Leave the raisins whole and then mix everything together, including the rum and Cyprus wine.
4. This is the filling; now it must be enclosed in puff pastry for which you can use the recipe from N. 155 in the proportion of 160 grams flour and 80 grams butter.
5. Roll it out tight, long and of a thickness a little more than a coin.
6. Spread the filling on it and make it into a roll like a sausage by pulling the pastry on the edges to join it.
7. Give it a circumference of about 10 centimeters, flatten it somewhat or leave it round, place it in a buttered copper pan coiled around itself like a serpent; but not too tight.
8. Finally, with a brush, coat it with a liquid mixture of melted butter and one egg yolk.
9. Instead of one you can make two, if you like, with this same dose, half of which I believe would be enough for seven or eight people.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Prugne giulebbate (Glazed Prunes)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Dried Bosnian prunes, 500 grams
- Good red wine, 4 deciliters
- Water, 2 deciliters
- Marsala, one small glass
- White sugar, 100 grams
- One piece of cinnamon

## Directions

1. Wash the prunes and soak them for two hours in fresh water
2. Remove them and dry them
3. Put them on the fire with the red wine, water, Marsala, sugar, and cinnamon
4. Boil them slowly for half an hour in a covered pot, which should be sufficient, but before removing them from the fire ascertain that they have softened enough, as more or less cooking may depend on the quality of the fruit
5. Remove them dried and place them in the vessel where you wish to serve them
6. Let the remaining syrup reduce on the fire for eight or ten minutes in an uncovered pot and then pour it also into the vessel over the prunes
7. Instead of the scent of cinnamon, you may substitute vanilla or the zest of citron or orange
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Pudding Cesarino
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Crumb of fine bread, 200 grams
- Sugar, 250 grams
- Sugar for the mold, approximately 100 grams
- Malaga raisins, 125 grams
- Sultana raisins, 125 grams
- Milk, half a liter
- Marsala and rum combined, three tablespoonfuls total
- Eggs, 5

## Directions

1. Cut the bread crumb into thin slices and throw it into the milk
2. While waiting for this to soak, clean the raisins and remove the seeds from the malaga variety and prepare the form for cooking, which should be the one for puddings, made of worked copper
3. Melt the aforementioned approximately 100 grams of sugar in a saucepan over heat and when it has taken on a hazelnut color, pour it into the mold to coat it completely; when it has cooled, grease the sugar coating with fresh butter
4. To the soaked crumb add the aforementioned 250 grams of sugar, the egg yolks and liqueurs, stirring everything well
5. Finally add the raisins and the beaten egg whites
6. Cook it in a water bath for three full hours, with heat underneath only in the last hour
7. Serve it hot and in flames and therefore water it abundantly with rum and set it alight with a spoonful of lit spirits
8. This will be enough for ten or twelve people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Quaglie col Riso (Quails with Rice)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Quails, 4
- Ham (prosciutto)
- Onion, 1/4
- Butter
- Salt and pepper
- Broth
- Rice, 400 grams
- Parmesan cheese

## Directions

1. Make a battuto with ham and a quarter onion
2. Put this in a pan with butter and cook until the onion is colored
3. Place clean, gutted, whole quails in the pan
4. Season with salt and pepper
5. Once browned, partially cook the quails with broth
6. Add rice and cook with enough broth until done, together with the quails
7. Season with Parmesan cheese when cooked
8. Serve either broth-rich or dry, as preferred, mixed with the quails
9. Serves four people
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Quagliette (Small Quail-Style Rolls)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal cutlets, filled according to recipe N. 307, or veal of milk
- A very thin slice of lard
- Twine
- Croutons
- Sage leaves
- Oil
- Salt
- Broth

## Directions

1. Use filled veal cutlets from recipe N. 307, or make the wrapping with milk veal; when they are filled, wrap them with a very thin slice of lard and tie them crosswise with twine.
2. Thread them onto the spit to cook as a roast, each one between two croutons and with some sage leaves, oil them, salt them, moisten them with a few spoonfuls of broth and unwrap them when you serve them.
3. You can also obtain an excellent roast with beef tenderloin cut into pieces, wrapped with lard, with the aroma of sage and between two croutons.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Quattro Quarti all'Inglese (English Pound Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 eggs and equal weight of sugar and flour (including eggshell weight)
- Raisins, 200 grams
- Butter, 200 grams
- Candied fruit cut into pieces, 30 grams
- Baking soda, one teaspoon

## Directions

1. Work the eggs with the sugar first, add the flour and continue working with a wooden spoon for approximately half an hour
2. Let the mixture rest for one or two hours, then add to it the butter melted in a bain-marie, the baking soda, the raisins and the candied fruit
3. Pour it into a baking pan or smooth mold, greased with butter and dusted with powdered sugar mixed with flour
4. Bake it in the oven
5. First wash the raisins to clean them from the earth they ordinarily contain, and dry them
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Quattro Quarti all'Italiana (Italian Pound Cake)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 eggs and equal weight of sugar and flour (including eggshell weight)
- Butter, 200 grams
- Baking soda, one teaspoon
- Lemon zest for flavoring
- Sweet almonds with a few bitter ones, 100 grams

## Directions

1. Make this cake in the same manner as the previous one except substitute candied fruit with lemon zest flavoring, and substitute 100 grams of sweet almonds with some bitter almonds for the raisins
2. After blanching the almonds, dry them in the sun or by the fire, pound them finely with two tablespoons of the sugar from the recipe and mix them with the flour before adding them to the mixture
3. This cake needs to be worked a great deal, both before and after adding the butter
4. Keep the bowl immersed in hot water while working for better results
5. Bake it in the oven
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Quenelles (French Dumplings)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- veal, 120 grams
- veal kidney fat, 80 grams
- flour, 50 grams
- butter, 30 grams
- eggs, one whole and one yolk
- milk, 2 deciliters
- salt
- tomato sauce (recipe N. 125)
- fresh or dried mushrooms
- green olives in brine
- butter, 30 grams
- flour, one tablespoon

## Directions

1. Clean the veal well of membranes and the fat of the skin that covers it, and after weighing them, mince them as much as possible with a knife and crescent-shaped knife, then pound them in a mortar until reduced to a very fine paste.
2. Make a béchamel with the flour, butter, and milk mentioned above, and when it has cooled, unite it with the pounded mixture, the eggs, and seasoning of only salt, and mix everything together well.
3. Spread a veil of flour on the work surface, pour the mixture over it, and lightly floured, roll it into a stick shape to obtain 18 or 20 pieces similar to sausages, about one finger long.
4. Put water on the fire in a wide pan, and when it boils throw in the quenelles; let them boil for 8 or 10 minutes and you will see them puff up.
5. With a slightly perforated spoon, remove them dry and serve them swimming in tomato sauce N. 125, to which you will add some fresh or dried mushrooms (which you will have cooked beforehand in the same sauce) and some olives in brine, from which you will remove the pit.
6. You can substitute the tomato sauce with meat gravy, or garnish with the quenelles an offal stew. They can also be made with the white meat of chickens or with fish flesh, and this quantity can be enough for five people.
7. If you use the tomato sauce, which is most suitable for this delicate-flavored dish, bind it with a mixture composed of 30 grams of butter and one tablespoon of flour, pouring it into it when it has taken on a hazelnut color on the fire.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ranocchi alla Fiorentina (Frogs Florentine Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- frogs
- flour
- oil
- salt
- pepper
- eggs
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Remove the frogs from fresh water where you have placed them after having held them for just a moment in hot water if they have just been killed. Dry them well between the folds of a cloth and coat them in flour. Put a pan on the fire with good oil and when it begins to sizzle throw in the frogs; season them with salt and pepper stirring often because they stick easily. When they are browned on both sides, pour over them beaten eggs, seasoned also with salt and pepper and lemon juice if you like; without touching them, let them set like an omelette and send the pan to table. The gall bladder should always be removed from the frogs. If you want them fried, coat them in flour and, before throwing them in the pan, keep them for a few hours in infusion in egg seasoned with salt and pepper; or, after coating in flour, brown them lightly on both sides and, taking one at a time, immerse them in egg seasoned with pepper, salt and lemon juice, putting them back in the pan to finish cooking them.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ranocchi in Umido (Stewed Frogs)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- frogs
- oil
- garlic
- parsley
- salt
- pepper
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. The simplest way is to make them with a soffritto of oil, garlic and parsley, salt and pepper, and when they are cooked, add lemon juice. Some use tomato juice instead of lemon, but the former is to be preferred. Never remove the eggs as they are the best part.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ravioli (Ravioli)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Ricotta, 300 grams
- Grated Parmesan, 50 grams
- Eggs, 2
- Cooked beet greens (bietola), as much as fits in a handful
- Scent of nutmeg and spices
- Salt, as needed
- Flour

## Directions

1. Pass the ricotta through a sieve, and if it is watery, squeeze it first in a cloth
2. Clean the beet greens of their stems, boil them without water, squeeze them well and chop them finely with the crescent blade
3. Make a paste of everything, take the mixture by spoonfuls and placing it on flour that you have spread on the work surface, wrap it well giving it the rounded and oblong shape of croquettes
4. With this amount you will make about two dozen ravioli
5. To cook them, throw them into unsalted water that is boiling hard and lift them out with the slotted spoon so they remain dry
6. Dress them either with sauce or with cheese and butter and serve them as a pasta course or as a side to a meat stew
7. Since cooking them is quick, it being enough that they set, cook them a few at a time so they do not break
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ravioli all'Uso di Romagna (Ravioli Romagna Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Ricotta, 150 grams
- Flour, 50 grams
- Grated Parmesan, 40 grams
- One egg and one yolk
- Salt, as needed

## Directions

1. Make everything into a paste and spread it on the work surface on a veil of flour to give it a cylindrical shape
2. Cut it into fourteen or fifteen equal pieces shaping them as needed
3. Boil them then for two or three minutes in unsalted water
4. Dress them with cheese and meat broth, or serve them as a side to a pot roast or a fricandeau
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ravioli alla Genovese (Ravioli Genoa Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Half breast of capon or young hen
- One lamb brain with some sweetbreads
- One chicken liver
- A small piece of butter
- Fat and lean prosciutto
- Few boiled and passed spinach
- Grated Parmesan
- Nutmeg
- Two egg yolks

## Directions

1. Put these things on the fire with a small piece of butter and when they begin to take color, finish cooking them with meat broth
2. Lift them out dry and chop them very finely with the crescent blade together with a slice of fat and lean prosciutto
3. Then add few boiled and passed spinach, grated Parmesan, nutmeg and two egg yolks
4. Mix and close them like cappelletti Romagna style or in a simpler way; with this amount you will make about sixty
5. Cook them in broth for a pasta course, or dry with cheese and butter, or with sauce
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ribes all'Inglese (Currants English Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Currants, 300 grams
- Sugar, 120 grams
- Water, 2 deciliters

## Directions

1. Clean the currants of their stems and put them on the fire with the said water
2. When it comes to a boil, add the sugar
3. Two minutes of boiling is enough, as the currants must remain whole
4. Pour it into a compote dish and serve it cold as cooked fruit
5. The seeds, if one does not wish to swallow them, can be sucked and spit out
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ribes all'inglese (English-Style Currants)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Currants, 300 grams
- Sugar, 120 grams
- Water, 2 deciliters

## Directions

1. Clean the currants of their stems
2. Put them on the fire with the said water
3. When it comes to a boil, pour in the sugar
4. Two minutes of boiling is enough, as the currants should remain whole
5. Pour it into a compote dish and serve it cold as cooked fruit
6. The seeds, if you do not wish to swallow them, can be sucked and spit out
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ribes alla Francese (Currants French Style)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Currants in their clusters, raw
- Gum arabic powder
- Water
- Crystalline powdered sugar, not confectioners' sugar

## Directions

1. Prepare a light solution of gum arabic powder dissolved in water
2. Take the raw currants in their little clusters with your fingers, dip them one at a time into the solution and dust them with crystalline powdered sugar, but not confectioners' sugar
3. Arrange them on a plate and this brilliant red base will make a fine display among the fruits of a meal and will be much appreciated by ladies
4. You may also intersperse red currants with white ones
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Ricciarelli di Siena (Sienese Almond Cookies)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- Fine white sugar, 220 grams
- Sweet almonds, 200 grams
- Bitter almonds, 20 grams
- Egg whites, 2
- Orange peel flavoring

## Directions

1. Blanch the almonds, dry them well in the sun or by the fire and pound them very finely in a mortar with two tablespoons of the sugar, poured in several times; then mix in the rest of the sugar.
2. Beat the egg whites in a bowl and pour in the almonds thus prepared and the grated orange peel. Mix again with a spoon and pour the mixture onto the work surface over a light layer of flour to take only the small amount of flour needed to lightly roll with a rolling pin into a soft sheet, half a finger thick.
3. Cut them with the form marked below and you will obtain 16 to 18 to cook in the following way:
4. Take a baking sheet, make a layer of bran as high as a shield and cover it all with wafers to place the ricciarelli on and bake them in the oven at moderate heat so they remain tender. In the absence of an oven, which would be most suitable, use a country oven.
5. After cooking, cut away the wafer that protrudes from the edges of these pastries, which turn out to be of fine quality.
source_title: La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene

title: Rifreddo di Vitella di Latte (Milk-fed Veal Cold Cut)
author: Pellegrino Artusi
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 boneless, all lean braciole of milk-fed veal, weighing about 400 grams
- 120 grams additional lean veal
- 50 grams of thick slice of cooked ham, fat and lean
- 20 grams additional cooked ham
- 50 grams of mortadella slice
- 30 grams grated Parmesan
- 20 grams butter
- 1 raw chicken breast
- 1 egg
- salt
- pepper
- 30 grams butter for cooking

## Directions

1. Dampen the braciole with water and beat it with a meat mallet to reduce it to about one centimeter thickness.
